META.subject: Biology
META.subtitleDoc: CBE Phenomenon-Driven Lesson Sequence — Sub-Strand 1.3 (8 Lessons)

UNIT.subject: Biology
UNIT.substrand: Sub-Strand 1.3: Cell Biology — Cell Structure and Specialisation
UNIT.content:
• Cell as the basic unit of life — historical background (Hooke, Schleiden, Schwann, Virchow)
• Types of cells: prokaryotic (bacteria such as E. coli) vs. eukaryotic (plant and animal cells)
• Ultra-structure of the animal cell: cell membrane, nucleus, mitochondria, ribosomes, endoplasmic reticulum, Golgi apparatus, lysosomes, centrioles, cytoplasm
• Ultra-structure of the plant cell: cell wall (cellulose), chloroplasts, large central vacuole, plasmodesmata — and how these differ from animal cells
• Cell organelles and their functions — detailed functional roles of each organelle
• Cell specialisation: how cells differentiate to perform specific functions (e.g., red blood cells, guard cells, root hair cells, muscle cells, nerve cells)
• Tissues, organs and organ systems — hierarchy from cell → tissue → organ → organ system → organism
• Comparison of plant and animal cells — similarities and differences in structure and function
UNIT.learningOutcomes:
By the end of the sub-strand, the learner should be able to:
a) describe the ultra-structure of plant and animal cells using diagrams and models,
b) identify and state the functions of cell organelles in plant and animal cells,
c) distinguish between prokaryotic and eukaryotic cells with reference to their structural features,
d) explain how cell specialisation enables cells to perform specific functions in living organisms,
e) construct a hierarchy from cell to organism, relating cell structure to tissue, organ and organ system organisation,
f) appreciate the relationship between cell structure and function in maintaining life processes.
UNIT.coreCompetencies:
• Communication and Collaboration: Learners work in small groups to construct annotated 3D cell models using locally available materials (bottle tops, avocado seeds, maize grains, plastic bags) and present their models to the class, justifying their choices of materials for each organelle.
• Critical Thinking and Problem Solving: Learners analyse electron micrograph images of different specialised cells (e.g., a Kenyan blood smear showing red blood cells, a maize leaf cross-section) and reason about why each cell's structure suits its function.
• Imagination and Creativity: Learners design and build physical or digital models of plant and animal cells, making creative decisions about how to represent organelles using everyday Kenyan materials found at home or in the market.
• Self-efficacy: Learners reflect on how understanding cell specialisation connects to real career pathways (medicine, agriculture, biotechnology) relevant to Kenya's development goals under Vision 2030.
UNIT.values:
• Responsibility: The learner responsibly handles microscopes and prepared slides of onion epidermis, cheek cells, or other specimens, ensuring equipment is cleaned and stored correctly after use.
• Respect: The learner appreciates diverse contributions from peers during group model-building activities and discussions about cell structure and function.
• Unity: The learner collaborates equitably in small groups during practical activities, ensuring all members participate in constructing and presenting cell models.
• Social Justice: The learner discusses how access to medical technologies rooted in cell biology (e.g., malaria diagnosis via blood smear microscopy — relevant in Kisumu and the Lake Victoria region) affects communities across Kenya.
UNIT.sep:
• SEP 1 — Asking Questions and Defining Problems: Learners generate and record investigable questions on the Driving Question Board (DQB) about why cells look different, e.g., "Why does a red blood cell have no nucleus but a cheek cell does?"
• SEP 2 — Developing and Using Models: Learners build annotated 3D models of plant and animal cells using locally available materials and revise them as understanding deepens across the 8-lesson sequence.
• SEP 4 — Analysing and Interpreting Data: Learners examine photomicrographs and electron micrographs of specialised cells (e.g., guard cells from a kale/sukuma wiki leaf, root hair cells from a bean plant) and identify structural adaptations.
• SEP 6 — Constructing Explanations: Learners write and discuss evidence-based explanations for how a cell's structure (e.g., the large surface area of a root hair cell) relates to its function (e.g., water and mineral absorption).
• SEP 8 — Obtaining, Evaluating and Communicating Information: Learners research Kenyan scientists in cell biology and biotechnology (e.g., researchers at KEMRI and JKUAT), evaluate sources for credibility, and present findings to peers.
UNIT.pcis:
• Health Education: Learners connect cell biology to understanding diseases prevalent in Kenya — e.g., how the malaria parasite (Plasmodium) invades and destroys red blood cells, linking cell structure to public health in endemic counties such as Kisumu and Homa Bay.
• Environmental Conservation: Learners discuss how plant cell structures (chloroplasts, cell walls) are fundamental to photosynthesis and food production, reinforcing the importance of conserving vegetation in Kenya's agroecological zones such as the Rift Valley and Central highlands.
• Safety and Security: Learners observe safe laboratory practices when preparing wet mount slides (e.g., of onion epidermal cells or cheek cells), handling glass coverslips, and using microscopes responsibly.
• Life Skills Education (Critical Thinking): Learners critically evaluate claims about cell-based products (e.g., stem cell therapies advertised in Kenyan media) using their knowledge of cell structure and specialisation.
• Career Guidance: Learners relate cell biology knowledge to careers in medicine, nursing, biotechnology, agriculture, and food science within the Kenyan job market and Kenya's Big Four Agenda priorities.
UNIT.careers:
• Medical Doctor / Clinical Officer: Understanding cell structure and specialisation underpins diagnosis and treatment of cellular diseases such as sickle cell anaemia (prevalent in western Kenya) and cancer.
• Medical Laboratory Technologist: Directly applies knowledge of cell types and microscopy (as practised at facilities like Kenyatta National Hospital) to diagnose diseases from blood smears and tissue samples.
• Agricultural Scientist / Agronomist: Uses knowledge of plant cell specialisation and tissue organisation to improve crop yields of staple foods like maize, beans, and sorghum grown across Kenya.
• Biotechnologist / Genetic Engineer: Applies cell biology principles at institutions such as KEMRI and JKUAT to develop GMO crops, vaccines, and diagnostic tools relevant to Kenya's health and food security goals.
• Nurse / Midwife: Applies understanding of human cell organisation and organ systems in patient care across Kenya's county hospitals and health centres.
• Food Scientist / Nutritionist: Relates cell and tissue biology to the nutritional quality and processing of Kenyan foods such as milk, meat, and fermented products like mursik.
UNIT.focus: This 8-lesson unit anchors learning in the puzzling observation that all living organisms — from a single-celled amoeba in a Nairobi pond to a maize plant in the Rift Valley to a Kenyan marathon runner — are built from the same fundamental unit: the cell, yet cells look radically different from one another. Learners move from examining familiar organisms to building detailed understanding of cell ultra-structure, organelle function, and specialisation, using models, microscopy, and data analysis. The unit consistently connects cell biology to Kenya-relevant health, agricultural, and biotechnology contexts, ensuring learning is grounded in students' lived experiences.
UNIT.phenomenon: ANCHORING PHENOMENON: A Form 4 student from Kisumu donates blood at the local county hospital. The medical laboratory technologist prepares a blood smear, stains it, and examines it under a microscope. The student is astonished to see that the red blood cells look like flattened biconcave discs with NO nucleus — yet the white blood cells right next to them have large, visible nuclei. Stranger still, when the same student chews a piece of sukuma wiki (kale) from their family's shamba and a teacher prepares a microscope slide of the leaf, the cells are rectangular, have thick green structures inside, and appear to have a large water-filled sac pushing everything to the side. HOW CAN ALL THESE STRUCTURES — a human blood cell, a white blood cell, a leaf cell — all be called "cells" yet look and behave so completely differently? What do they share? What is different, and WHY? This puzzle drives the entire unit: students must build a model of what a "generic" cell looks like, then explain how and why specialised cells deviate from that model to perform specific jobs in the body and in plants.
UNIT.supportingPhenomena:
• SUPPORTING PHENOMENON 1 — The Fermenting Uji: A family in Kisii leaves a pot of uji (fermented porridge) uncovered overnight. By morning it smells sour and has bubbles. Under a microscope, tiny single-celled organisms (yeast and bacteria) are visible moving and dividing. Students ask: are these the same kind of "cell" as in our bodies? (Introduces prokaryote vs. eukaryote distinction.)
• SUPPORTING PHENOMENON 2 — The Wilting Bean Plant: A bean plant (common across Kenyan smallholder farms) is left without water for two days and wilts dramatically. When watered, it recovers within hours. Students observe that plant cells have a large central vacuole that fills with water — what role does this organelle play, and why don't animal cells behave the same way?
• SUPPORTING PHENOMENON 3 — The Sickle Cell Crisis: A student reads a news report about a child from western Kenya being admitted to hospital during a "sickle cell crisis." Photographs show that the child's red blood cells are sickle-shaped instead of biconcave disc-shaped and are blocking small blood vessels. Students ask: how does a change in cell shape — at the microscopic level — cause so much pain and organ damage?
• SUPPORTING PHENOMENON 4 — The Root Hair Close-Up: A freshly pulled bean seedling from the school garden shows tiny, hair-like projections on the roots visible to the naked eye. Under a microscope these are revealed to be single elongated cells. Students ask: why are these cells so long and thin, and how does their shape help the plant absorb water from Kenyan red soil?
UNIT.drivingQuestion:
DRIVING QUESTION: "Why do the cells in my body — and in the sukuma wiki on my plate — look so different from each other, and how does each cell's structure allow it to do its specific job?"

KICD KEY INQUIRY QUESTIONS:
1. What are the structural differences between plant and animal cells?
2. How does cell specialisation enable different cells to perform specific functions in living organisms?
UNIT.storylineThread:
Lesson 1 — PREDICT: "What is a cell?" | Students observe the anchoring phenomenon (blood smear image + sukuma wiki leaf image side by side). Without prior instruction, students sketch what they think a "basic cell" looks like and predict: what structures must ALL cells share? Predictions posted to the Driving Question Board (DQB). Teacher introduces the historical thread: Hooke, Schleiden, Schwann, Virchow — why did it take so long for humans to discover cells?

Lesson 2 — OBSERVE (Microscopy Practical 1): "Seeing cells for the first time" | Students prepare wet mount slides of onion epidermal cells AND their own cheek cells (cotton bud method, safely). They observe, draw, and label what they see under low and high power. Students record DIFFERENCES between the two cell types. DQB updated: new questions added about structures they cannot yet explain (e.g., "What is the green stuff in the plant cell?").

Lesson 3 — EXPLAIN (Organelle Structure & Function — Animal Cell): "What is inside the cell doing all the work?" | Using electron micrograph images and annotated diagrams, learners systematically identify and learn the function of each animal cell organelle — nucleus, mitochondria, ribosomes, ER, Golgi, lysosomes, centrioles. Students link each organelle to a real-life factory analogy set in a Nairobi industrial park (nucleus = managing director's office; mitochondria = generator room; etc.). Students return to DQB and answer early questions.

Lesson 4 — EXPLAIN (Organelle Structure & Function — Plant Cell): "How is a plant cell like and unlike an animal cell?" | Focus on plant-specific organelles: chloroplasts (photosynthesis in sukuma wiki leaves), cell wall (rigidity and wilting phenomenon), central vacuole (turgor pressure and the wilting bean supporting phenomenon). Students complete a structured comparison table. Model-building begins: students start constructing labelled 2D diagrams of both cell types.

Lesson 5 — OBSERVE + EXPLAIN (Prokaryote vs. Eukaryote): "Are bacteria cells the same as our cells?" | Revisit the fermenting uji supporting phenomenon. Students examine images/diagrams of bacterial cells (E. coli) vs. human cells. Identify key differences: no membrane-bound nucleus, no membrane-bound organelles, smaller size, presence of cell wall in bacteria. Students update their DQB and revise their models to add a "prokaryote" column.

Lesson 6 — EXPLAIN (Cell Specialisation): "Why don't all cells look the same?" | Students investigate 6 specialised cells: red blood cells (no nucleus → more haemoglobin space), guard cells (bean-shaped → control stomatal opening in sukuma wiki), root hair cells (elongated → large surface area in bean seedling), muscle cells (elongated with many mitochondria → contraction), nerve cells (long axons → signal transmission), and sperm cells (flagellum → motility). Students match structural features to functions using evidence cards. Sickle cell supporting phenomenon is revisited: students explain why the abnormal shape disrupts function.

Lesson 7 — MODEL BUILDING + DQB Revision: "Building our complete cell model" | Students construct annotated 3D models of a plant OR animal cell using locally available materials (avocado seed = nucleus, plastic bag = cell membrane, bottle tops = mitochondria, green paper = chloroplasts, maize grain = ribosomes, etc.). Groups present models, explain organelle choices, and peer-assess. DQB is formally revisited: which questions have now been answered? Which remain? Students record remaining questions as "bridges" to future sub-strands (Chemicals of Life, Plant Physiology).

Lesson 8 — DRIVING QUESTION SYNTHESIS + Assessment: "So why DO cells look so different?" | Students return to the original anchoring phenomenon (blood smear + sukuma wiki leaf). Each student writes a full evidence-based explanation answering the driving question, referencing: (a) shared organelles all cells have, (b) organelles unique to plant or animal cells, (c) how specialisation removes or modifies organelles for a specific function, and (d) one Kenya-relevant example (sickle cell, malaria parasite, root hair absorption). Class constructs a final consensus model on the board. Teacher previews Sub-Strand 1.4 (Chemicals of Life): "Now that we know what cells look like — what are they actually MADE of?"

LESSONS.title: What Is a Cell? Launching the Phenomenon — Blood, Sukuma Wiki, and the Mystery of Life's Building Blocks

slo.purpose: To launch the anchoring phenomenon and elicit students' prior ideas about cell structure, establishing the investigative question that will drive the entire unit.
slo.knowledge: By the end of this lesson, the learner should be able to: (a) state that all living organisms are made of cells; (b) identify that cells can vary dramatically in shape, size, and internal structures while still being classified as cells; (c) recall key milestones in the historical development of the cell theory (Hooke, Schleiden, Schwann, Virchow).
slo.skills: By the end of this lesson, the learner should be able to: (a) sketch and annotate a predicted 'basic cell' diagram based on prior knowledge and observation; (b) formulate testable questions arising from the anchoring phenomenon; (c) record observations from microscope images systematically using a structured observation table.
slo.attitudes: The learner appreciates that scientific understanding is built incrementally over time through the curiosity and collaborative work of many scientists, and values asking questions as the starting point of inquiry.
slo.keyInquiry: 1. What are the structural differences between plant and animal cells? 2. How does cell specialisation enable different cells to perform specific functions in living organisms?
slo.purposeInStoryline: This is the OPENING lesson of the unit. Its sole purpose is to create productive confusion — students encounter the anchoring phenomenon (blood smear vs. sukuma wiki leaf cell images) and realise they cannot yet explain why cells of the same organism, or across organisms, look so radically different. All predictions and questions generated here are posted to the Driving Question Board (DQB) and will be revisited and revised across all 6 lessons of the sub-strand. No definitive answers are given in this lesson; the goal is to surface prior knowledge, generate curiosity, and establish the investigative frame.
slo.safetyNotes: If physical microscopes and prepared slides are used during the Observe phase: (1) Handle glass slides with care — report any breakage to the teacher immediately; do not attempt to pick up glass shards. (2) Do not touch the microscope lens with fingers. (3) If staining reagents (e.g., iodine) are available, avoid contact with skin and eyes; wash hands after use. (4) No safety concerns arise from viewing projected digital images. Teacher should establish lab safety norms at the start of the lesson if conducted in the laboratory.

LESSONS.overview: Students are confronted — without prior instruction — with two side-by-side microscope images: (1) a stained human blood smear showing biconcave, anucleate red blood cells alongside nucleated white blood cells, and (2) a microscope image of a sukuma wiki (kale) leaf cell showing rectangular shape, chloroplasts, a large central vacuole, and a cell wall. The lesson opens with a silent observation, followed by individual prediction sketches of what a 'basic cell' looks like. Student predictions are shared, debated, and posted to the newly created Driving Question Board (DQB). The teacher then introduces the historical thread — Hooke, Schleiden, Schwann, and Virchow — to show that even professional scientists took centuries to agree on what a cell is. The lesson closes with students writing their best current question about cells on a sticky note for the DQB. No formal definitions or diagrams are provided by the teacher; understanding is built through student sensemaking of the phenomenon.

framework.learnerExperience: Students enter to find two large projected images displayed silently on the screen — no labels, no title. Image A shows a stained blood smear (red blood cells and white blood cells visible). Image B shows a sukuma wiki leaf cell under a microscope (chloroplasts, large central vacuole, rectangular shape, cell wall visible). Students are given 3 minutes of silent, individual 'Notice and Wonder' time. Each student writes in their exercise book: at least 3 things they NOTICE and at least 2 things they WONDER. After silent time, students share with a shoulder partner (2 min). Each student then independently sketches — in pencil, in their exercise books — what they believe a 'basic cell' (a cell that could represent ALL cells) looks like, and labels any structures they think must be present. Beneath their sketch, they write: 'I predict all cells must have: ___' and 'I predict cells can be different because: ___'. Predictions are NOT corrected by the teacher at this stage.
framework.teacherMoves: As students enter, teacher stands silently at the front, gesturing to the projected images — do NOT introduce or explain. After 3 minutes, say: 'Without talking to anyone, write in your book — three things you notice, two things you wonder. You have 3 minutes. Go.' [START 3-MIN TIMER VISIBLY ON BOARD.] Circulate silently; do not answer questions. After sharing time, say: 'Now I want you to do something bold. Without me teaching you anything — draw what YOU think a basic cell looks like. Label everything you believe is inside. You have 5 minutes.' [START 5-MIN TIMER.] Cold-call 4 students to share their sketches by holding books up or drawing on the board. Use these exact prompts — to student 1: 'Talk us through your drawing — what did you include and WHY?' [WAIT 10 SECONDS after the question before accepting response.] To student 2: 'What is ONE thing your drawing has that [Student 1]'s does not?' [WAIT 10 SECONDS.] To student 3: 'Look at Image A on the board — the blood cells. Do those cells match YOUR drawing? What is different?' [WAIT 15 SECONDS.] To student 4: 'Look at Image B — the sukuma wiki cell. What does that cell have that surprises you most?' [WAIT 15 SECONDS.] Teacher records ALL proposed cell structures on the board in two columns: 'We agree all cells have:' | 'We are NOT sure about:'. Do NOT resolve disagreements — preserve productive tension.
framework.sensemakingStrategy: NOTICE AND WONDER + PREDICT-BEFORE-INSTRUCTION: Students activate prior knowledge and generate personal hypotheses before encountering any new information. This strategy surfaces misconceptions (e.g., 'all cells are round', 'all cells have a nucleus') that the teacher can deliberately address in later lessons. By making predictions public, students become personally invested in testing and revising them — the core of CBE inquiry. The two-column class record externalises the class's collective prior knowledge and creates a visible 'gap' that motivates learning.
framework.formativeAssessment: OBSERVABLE INDICATOR: Every student has a labelled cell sketch in their exercise book with at least one written prediction sentence. Teacher circulates and looks for: (1) presence or absence of nucleus in student sketches — a key diagnostic for later misconception resolution; (2) whether students distinguish between plant and animal cell features in Image A vs. B; (3) quality of 'wonder' questions — surface-level ('why is it green?') vs. structural ('why does one cell have a thick border and the other doesn't?'). Teacher mentally notes 2–3 students with particularly generative questions to cold-call during the DQB phase. No marks awarded; this is diagnostic only.

framework.learnerExperience: Students receive a structured OBSERVATION TABLE (printed or copied from the board) with three columns: 'Feature Observed', 'Blood Cell (Red)', 'Blood Cell (White)', 'Sukuma Wiki Leaf Cell'. Teacher projects each image in turn at high resolution. Students complete the table systematically — describing shape, colour after staining, visible internal structures, and approximate relative size. After completing their own tables individually (8 minutes), students compare with a group of 4 and reach a consensus table on one shared piece of paper. Groups are then directed to circle THREE features that appear in ALL three cell types — these are candidate 'universal cell features'. Groups also star ONE feature that appears in ONLY ONE of the three cell types — their biggest anomaly. A class gallery-walk is simulated: each group reads out their three circled 'universal' features. Teacher tallies on the board which features are most commonly agreed upon across groups. Students then read a short 1-page historical narrative (provided on paper or screen) titled: 'The Long Road to the Cell: From Hooke's Cork to Virchow's Omnis cellula e cellula' — covering Robert Hooke (1665, cork cells, Micrographia), Matthias Schleiden (1838, plant cells), Theodor Schwann (1839, animal cells), Rudolf Virchow (1855, cells come from cells). Students annotate the reading by underlining: in blue — 'what the scientist observed', in red — 'what conclusion they drew'.
framework.teacherMoves: Project Image A (blood smear) first. Say: 'You have 4 minutes to fill in the first two columns of your observation table — describe exactly what you SEE, not what you think you know. Be a detective, not a textbook.' [START 4-MIN TIMER.] Then project Image B (sukuma wiki). Say: 'Now 4 minutes for the sukuma wiki leaf cell column.' [START 4-MIN TIMER.] During group comparison: 'Your group has 3 minutes. Find THREE features you all agree appear in every single cell on this board. Find ONE feature that is strangest — that only one type of cell seems to have.' [CIRCULATE — listen for the word 'nucleus'; if a group says 'the red blood cell has no nucleus', crouch to their level and say: 'That is a fascinating observation. Write that down exactly. We are going to chase that question all unit long.'] During the historical reading: 'As you read, I want you to be thinking about one question: WHY did it take from 1665 to 1855 — almost 200 years — for scientists to agree that ALL living things are made of cells? What was making it so hard?' After reading, cold-call 3 students: Student 1 — 'What did Robert Hooke actually see when he looked at cork?' [WAIT 10 SECONDS.] Student 2 — 'Schwann worked with animal tissue. Why do you think it was harder to see animal cells than plant cells?' [WAIT 15 SECONDS — this is a higher-order question; accept all reasonable answers.] Student 3 — 'Virchow said Omnis cellula e cellula — every cell from a cell. How does that idea connect to the blood smear image we opened class with?' [WAIT 15 SECONDS.]
framework.sensemakingStrategy: STRUCTURED OBSERVATION TABLE + ANNOTATED READING: The observation table forces students to move from vague impressions to precise, feature-level descriptions — a core NGSS Science and Engineering Practice (SEP 3: Planning and Carrying Out Investigations; SEP 4: Analysing and Interpreting Data). The annotated reading (observation vs. conclusion) models the distinction between empirical evidence and scientific inference — a critical epistemological habit. The historical narrative contextualises the difficulty of the problem: if brilliant scientists took 200 years, students should expect the unit's central question to be genuinely hard and worth pursuing.
framework.formativeAssessment: OBSERVABLE INDICATOR: Completed observation tables with entries in all columns. Teacher looks for: (1) Whether students record 'no nucleus' for red blood cells — this is the highest-value observation and will be the anchor for the specialisation lessons. (2) Whether students use descriptive language ('irregular, lobed nucleus' for white blood cells) vs. vague language ('has stuff inside'). (3) During cold-call on Virchow: can students connect 'cells from cells' to the idea that blood cells were once produced by dividing stem cells — a connection that previews specialisation? Award a visible group tally mark on the board to the first group that correctly identifies 'cell membrane' as a universal feature — this gamifies the observation without grading it.

framework.learnerExperience: Teacher leads a whole-class 'Claim — Evidence — Reasoning' (CER) activity. Using only the evidence gathered in Phase 2 (observation tables and historical reading — NO new information introduced), each student writes a CER response in their exercise book to the prompt: 'CLAIM: Based on our observations today, I think all cells must have at least ___ [name the structures]. EVIDENCE: I know this because in the images we observed, I could see ___. REASONING: This matters because if a structure is in every cell type we looked at, then ___.' After writing individually (5 minutes), teacher cold-calls 3 students to read their claims aloud. Class votes (thumbs up / thumbs sideways / thumbs down) on whether each claim is supported by the evidence shown today. Teacher scribes the most evidence-supported claim on a large sheet of paper labelled 'OUR BEST CURRENT EXPLANATION — Lesson 1'. This is visibly posted on the classroom wall. Teacher then introduces the formal driving question of the unit verbally (does NOT answer it): 'Why do the cells in my body — and in the sukuma wiki on my plate — look so different from each other, and how does each cell's structure allow it to do its specific job?' Students write this question at the top of a new page in their books — this is their personal unit driving question page.
framework.teacherMoves: Say: 'We have not read a textbook today. We have not copied a definition. But you have been doing real science — observing, noticing, wondering, reading primary sources. Now I want you to make a CLAIM based only on what WE found in this room today. You have 5 minutes to write your CER. Go.' [START 5-MIN TIMER. CIRCULATE — if a student writes 'all cells have a nucleus', do not correct; instead ask: 'Are you sure? Look back at your observation table for the red blood cell column.' If a student writes 'all cells have a cell membrane', say quietly: 'That is a strong claim — make sure your evidence column says WHERE you saw evidence of that in the images.'] After 5 minutes, cold-call 3 students. After each student reads their claim, say to the class: 'Thumbs up if you think the evidence we gathered today supports this claim. Thumbs sideways if you are not sure. Thumbs down if you think today's evidence contradicts this claim.' [COUNT the thumbs visibly — 'I see 12 up, 8 sideways, 2 down — interesting. Why might 2 people say thumbs down?'] After posting 'Our Best Current Explanation', say: 'This paper will change. Every lesson in this unit, we will come back and either confirm, revise, or completely rewrite what it says. Science is not finished — and neither are we.'
framework.sensemakingStrategy: CLAIM — EVIDENCE — REASONING (CER): CER is a structured argumentation strategy (NGSS SEP 6: Constructing Explanations; SEP 7: Engaging in Argument from Evidence) that prevents students from restating textbook knowledge and forces them to ground claims in the specific evidence generated in THIS lesson. The public posting of 'Our Best Current Explanation' externalises the class's current model and creates accountability for revision — students can physically see their thinking evolve across the unit. The thumbs voting makes reasoning social and visible without being threatening.
framework.formativeAssessment: OBSERVABLE INDICATOR: Every student has a written CER in their exercise book. Teacher evaluates: (1) Does the CLAIM name specific structures (e.g., 'cell membrane', 'cytoplasm') or remain vague ('cells have stuff inside')? (2) Does the EVIDENCE column cite the specific images or historical reading rather than general knowledge? (3) Does the REASONING connect to the phenomenon — i.e., does the student gesture toward the fact that red blood cells challenge any claim about nuclei? Students whose CER identifies the contradiction between 'all cells have a nucleus' (common prior belief) and the anucleate red blood cell observation are flagged mentally by the teacher as ready for extension in Lesson 2.

framework.learnerExperience: Each student receives two sticky notes (or two small pieces of paper if sticky notes unavailable). On sticky note 1 (one colour — e.g., yellow): students write ONE question they still cannot answer about cells after today's lesson. On sticky note 2 (a different colour — e.g., blue): students write ONE prediction they made at the start of class that they now think might be WRONG and why. Students bring their sticky notes to the front and place them on the class Driving Question Board (DQB) — a large sheet of paper or a section of the classroom wall divided into columns: 'Questions about Cell Structure' | 'Questions about Why Cells Look Different' | 'Questions about How Cells Work'. Teacher helps students categorise their sticky notes into the correct column as they come forward (this takes 3–4 minutes as students arrive in waves). Teacher reads 3–4 questions aloud from the board — choosing the most generative ones — and says: 'These questions are going to drive everything we do for the next 5 lessons. I am not going to answer them now. By Lesson 6, you will answer them yourselves — with evidence.' Teacher adds ONE teacher-generated question to the DQB: 'Why does a red blood cell have NO nucleus — and is that a defect, or is it actually a design feature that makes it better at its job?'
framework.teacherMoves: Distribute sticky notes and say: 'Two notes. Two minutes. Note one — yellow: your single most burning question about cells that you cannot answer yet. Note two — blue: one prediction you made at the START of class that today's evidence made you doubt. Be honest — changing your mind is what scientists do.' [START 2-MIN TIMER.] As students post notes, circulate and read aloud strong questions as they go up: 'Listen to this question from [student name] — [read question]. Does anyone else have a similar question? Nod if you do.' After all notes are posted: 'This board is alive. It grows every lesson. Every time we answer a question, we cross it off — in green. Every time answering one question gives us a new question, we add it — in red. By the end of this unit, I expect this board to be covered in both green cross-outs and red new questions.' Point to the teacher-added question about the red blood cell nucleus and say: 'This one question — I promise you — is going to unlock everything about how cells become specialised. Hold it in your mind.'
framework.sensemakingStrategy: DRIVING QUESTION BOARD (DQB): The DQB is a metacognitive tool (NGSS SEP 1: Asking Questions) that makes student thinking visible, persistent, and revisable across the unit. By categorising questions into structural, comparative, and functional columns, the teacher scaffolds the epistemological organisation of biology — structure, variation, function — which mirrors the actual architecture of the KICD Cell Biology sub-strand. The two-colour sticky note system (questions vs. revised predictions) ensures that even the Predict phase is formally honoured and tracked, reinforcing that prediction and revision are core scientific practices, not just warm-ups.
framework.formativeAssessment: OBSERVABLE INDICATOR: Every student has posted two sticky notes. Teacher photographs the DQB at the end of the lesson (or keeps it intact) for comparison in Lesson 6. Diagnostic value: (1) Yellow questions clustering around 'why do cells look different' signal students are already thinking functionally — ready for specialisation content. (2) Yellow questions clustering around 'what is inside a cell' signal students need more structural scaffolding in Lessons 2–3. (3) Blue 'revised prediction' notes that say 'I thought all cells had a nucleus but the red blood cell doesn't' indicate the phenomenon has done its job — the anomaly has registered cognitively.

framework.learnerExperience: Students return to the cell sketch they drew in Phase 1 (Predict). Using a different coloured pen, they now REVISE their sketch in light of everything observed and discussed in the lesson. This is NOT a copy of a textbook diagram — students use only what they have built through the lesson's evidence. Revised sketches must include: (a) at least one structure they are NOW confident all cells share, labelled with a sentence explaining WHY they believe it is universal; (b) at least one structure they are now UNSURE about, marked with a '?' and a note explaining the uncertainty; (c) an annotation in the margin: 'This model is INCOMPLETE because I still do not know ___'. Students also write a brief 'Model Changelog' entry (2 sentences) beneath their revised sketch: 'I changed ___ from my original drawing because the evidence showed ___.' Teacher collects books at the end of the lesson (or photographs the page) so that the Lesson 1 model can be compared with the Lesson 6 final model — making growth of understanding visible.
framework.teacherMoves: Say: 'Return to your Phase 1 sketch — the one you drew before I taught you anything. Pick up a [different colour] pen. You are not throwing your first drawing away — you are revising it. Scientists never throw away their first model. They improve it.' [START 7-MIN TIMER for revision.] Circulate and ask targeted questions to 3–4 students: 'What are you adding that you did not have before — and what evidence from today tells you to add it?' [WAIT 10 SECONDS.] 'What are you leaving as a question mark — and WHY are you not sure?' [WAIT 10 SECONDS.] 'Your sukuma wiki leaf cell has a very thick border. Is that in your model? Should it be in a GENERIC cell model?' [WAIT 15 SECONDS — this seeds the plant vs. animal cell distinction for Lesson 3.] After 7 minutes: 'Write your Model Changelog — two sentences. I changed ___ because ___. You have 2 minutes.' After 2 minutes, cold-call 2 students to read their changelog aloud. Collect books or photograph the model page. Close the lesson by returning to the projected phenomenon images: 'We started this lesson with these two images. We end this lesson with the same two images — but now you see more than you did 80 minutes ago. Next lesson, we zoom in. We will look at the actual structures inside these cells and ask: what is each one built for?' Do NOT summarise or provide answers — leave the productive tension intact.
framework.sensemakingStrategy: ITERATIVE MODEL BUILDING (NGSS SEP 2: Developing and Using Models): By returning to and physically revising the Phase 1 sketch using a different colour, students make their conceptual change visible on the page. The 'Model Changelog' formalises the process of evidence-based revision — a metacognitive habit that is central to scientific practice and to CBE's emphasis on self-assessment. The deliberate incompleteness of the model ('This model is incomplete because ___') prevents premature closure and sustains curiosity across the unit. Comparing Lesson 1 and Lesson 6 models at the end of the unit provides a powerful, concrete artefact of learning progression for both students and the teacher.
framework.formativeAssessment: OBSERVABLE INDICATOR: Each student's revised sketch shows visible changes from the Phase 1 sketch (different colour additions, question marks, margin annotations). Teacher evaluates the Model Changelog for evidence of genuine evidence-based reasoning — 'I changed the nucleus to a question mark because the red blood cell image showed no nucleus' scores higher than 'I changed it because the teacher said so.' EXIT CHECK: Before leaving, each student holds up their book open to their revised sketch — teacher does a rapid visual scan. Any student whose revised sketch is identical to their Phase 1 sketch is individually asked: 'What is one thing from the sukuma wiki image that surprised you today?' to probe whether the observation registered. Lesson 1 model pages are kept intact in books — students are explicitly told: 'Do NOT erase or improve this after class. It is a record of where your thinking is today.'

LESSONS.teacherReflection:
After the lesson, reflect on the following questions:
1. PHENOMENON UPTAKE: Did the blood smear + sukuma wiki images create genuine confusion and curiosity, or did students seem uninterested? If uptake was low, consider: was the silent observation time long enough? Should the images be introduced with a brief narrative hook — e.g., 'Imagine you are the Form 4 student from Kisumu seeing your own blood cells for the first time...'?
2. PREDICTION QUALITY: Review the Phase 1 sketches. What proportion of students drew a nucleus? What proportion drew a cell membrane? What proportion drew organelles they may have encountered informally (mitochondria, chloroplasts)? This data tells you what scaffolding Lessons 2 and 3 must prioritise.
3. DQB RICHNESS: Photograph the DQB and count questions by category. If the majority of questions are in 'Questions about Cell Structure' (structural) rather than 'Questions about Why Cells Look Different' (functional), you may need to build a bridging activity at the start of Lesson 2 that pushes students toward functional thinking earlier.
4. RED BLOOD CELL ANOMALY: Did most groups identify the absence of a nucleus in red blood cells independently, or did it go unnoticed? If fewer than half the groups noticed it, consider adding a zoomed-in inset image of the red blood cell with an arrow pointing to the region where a nucleus would be expected, labelled 'Where is the nucleus?' — for use from Lesson 2 onward.
5. HISTORICAL THREAD: Were students genuinely engaged with the Hooke-to-Virchow reading, or did it feel like an interruption to the phenomenon work? If disconnected, consider reframing the reading as a 'scientist detective file' and asking: 'Which of these scientists would have been most useful to have in the lab with us today?'
6. EQUITY CHECK: Which students did NOT speak during cold-calls or group discussion? Note their names and create deliberate entry points for them in Lesson 2's structured pair work.

summaryTablePrompt.observed: Students observed two microscope images side by side: a stained human blood smear showing biconcave anucleate red blood cells and nucleated white blood cells, and a sukuma wiki (kale) leaf cell showing a rectangular shape, chloroplasts, a large central vacuole, and a visible cell wall. Students completed a structured observation table comparing features across three cell types.
summaryTablePrompt.learned: Students learned that cells can differ dramatically in shape, size, and internal structures while still being classified as cells. They were introduced to the historical development of cell theory through Hooke (1665), Schleiden (1838), Schwann (1839), and Virchow (1855), and practised distinguishing between scientific observations and the conclusions drawn from them.
summaryTablePrompt.explained: Students used Claim—Evidence—Reasoning (CER) to argue — based only on today's image evidence — which structures they believe are universal to all cells. They revised their initial 'generic cell' sketch using a different colour to show evidence-based changes, recorded a Model Changelog, and posted questions and revised predictions to the newly created Driving Question Board (DQB).

LESSONS.aresKeywords: cell biology, cell structure, plant cell, animal cell, red blood cell, white blood cell, sukuma wiki, chloroplast, vacuole, cell wall, nucleus, cell membrane, cell theory, Robert Hooke, Schleiden, Schwann, Virchow, microscopy, blood smear, specialisation, anchoring phenomenon, driving question board, predict observe explain, CER, model building, Grade 10 Biology, Kenya CBC, KICD, cell biology Kenya

LESSONS.title: Seeing Cells for the First Time: Microscopy Practical 1
LESSONS.aresKeywords: cell observation, wet mount preparation, onion epidermal cells, cheek cells, microscopy, biological drawing, cell structures, plant cell, animal cell

slo.purpose: By the end of this lesson, learners will have directly observed real plant and animal cells under a microscope, recorded accurate biological drawings, and identified visible structural differences between the two cell types, advancing their investigation of the anchoring phenomenon.
slo.knowledge: Learners will identify visible cell structures including the cell wall, cell membrane, nucleus, cytoplasm, and vacuole in onion epidermal cells and cheek cells as seen under light microscopy.
slo.skills: Learners will prepare wet mount slides using the correct technique, focus a light microscope under low and high power, produce labelled biological drawings that follow accepted conventions (sharp pencil, scale, labels with ruled lines), and record comparative observations in a structured table.
slo.attitudes: Learners will demonstrate curiosity and precision as they observe real biological material, appreciate that scientific evidence comes from careful direct observation, and show responsibility in handling laboratory materials safely and hygienically.
slo.keyInquiry: What are the structural differences between plant and animal cells?
slo.purposeInStoryline: This lesson is the first OBSERVE phase in the evidence-gathering sequence. Learners move beyond predictions made in Lesson 1 and gather direct observational evidence about real cells. The onion cells serve as an accessible plant cell proxy, while cheek cells represent human animal cells — connecting directly to the blood smear phenomenon. New unexplained observations (no green colour in onion, no nucleus visible in some cheek cells, the box-like walls of onion cells) are harvested onto the Driving Question Board to fuel curiosity for Lessons 3 and 4.
slo.safetyNotes: Learners must not share cotton buds between individuals. Each learner uses their own clean cotton bud for cheek cell collection and discards it immediately into a disinfectant beaker (dilute Jik solution) after use. Slides containing cheek cells are treated as biohazardous and placed in a disinfectant tray after observation. Learners wash hands before and after the practical. No learner with an open mouth sore participates in cheek swabbing — they observe a partner slide instead. Glass slides and cover slips are handled with care; broken glass is reported immediately to the teacher and not touched with bare hands.

LESSONS.overview: Learners open the lesson by revisiting their Lesson 1 predictions about what a basic cell looks like. They then conduct a hands-on microscopy practical in which they prepare wet mount slides of onion epidermal cells (plant cell) and their own cheek cells (animal cell). Using low and high power magnification, they observe, sketch, and label what they see, then complete a structured comparison table to record differences. The lesson closes with a Driving Question Board update in which learners post new questions arising from structures they observed but cannot yet explain — for example, the rectangular walls of onion cells, the faint or invisible nucleus in cheek cells, and the absence of any green colour in the onion layer used. These unresolved questions serve as the engine for Lessons 3 and 4.

framework.learnerExperience: Learners open their notebooks and spend 3 minutes individually reviewing the sketch of a basic cell they drew in Lesson 1. The teacher projects the two anchoring phenomenon images side by side: the stained blood smear (showing red blood cells and white blood cells) and the sukuma wiki leaf cell image. Learners are asked to circle ONE structure on their Lesson 1 sketch that they predict they will be able to see with a light microscope in an onion cell today, and ONE structure they predict they will see in their own cheek cell. Learners share predictions with a shoulder partner using Think-Pair language: 'I predict I will see ______ in the onion cell because ______.'
framework.teacherMoves: Teacher projects the anchoring phenomenon images and says: 'Last lesson you predicted what a basic cell looks like. Today we stop predicting and start seeing. But before we look through the microscope, tell me — what do you EXPECT to see in an onion cell versus in your cheek cell? They are both cells, so what might be the same? What might be different? Take 60 seconds in silence to look at your Lesson 1 sketch and circle your prediction.' [WAIT TIME: 60 seconds of silent individual thinking before any pair talk.] After pairs share, teacher cold-calls 3 pairs and records their predictions on the board in two columns: ONION CELL and CHEEK CELL. Teacher says: 'Keep these predictions visible. At the end of the lesson we will return to them and decide — were we right, partially right, or completely surprised?'
framework.sensemakingStrategy: Predictions are anchored to the students own prior drawings from Lesson 1, creating cognitive continuity. Recording predictions publicly on the board creates accountability and a reference point for end-of-lesson comparison, reinforcing the PREDICT-OBSERVE-EXPLAIN cycle explicitly.
framework.formativeAssessment: Teacher scans circled predictions on learner sketches during pair talk. Looks for evidence of recall from Lesson 1 — do learners mention nucleus, cell membrane, cytoplasm? Misconceptions to note: learners who predict they will see chloroplasts in the onion cell (onion epidermal cells lack chloroplasts — this will become a productive surprise). Teacher notes these learners to revisit during observation debrief.

framework.learnerExperience: Learners work in pairs at benches. Each pair has: 2 glass slides, 2 cover slips, iodine solution in a dropper bottle, a fresh onion, forceps or a ruler edge, cotton buds, methylene blue solution in a dropper bottle, tissue paper, and a light microscope. PART A — Onion Epidermal Cell: Learners peel a thin transparent layer from between two onion scales using forceps (the teacher demonstrates this first). The tissue is placed on a slide with one drop of water, flattened, and a cover slip lowered at an angle to avoid air bubbles. One drop of iodine solution is drawn under the cover slip using tissue paper on the opposite side. Learners focus under low power (4x or 10x objective) then switch to high power (40x objective). They observe and draw what they see in a circle representing the field of view, label what they can identify, and write the magnification used. PART B — Cheek Cell: Each learner gently rubs the inside of their own cheek with their own clean cotton bud, smears the material onto their own slide, adds one drop of methylene blue, covers with a cover slip, and observes. They draw and label what they see. Learners then complete a structured comparison table: FEATURE | ONION CELL | CHEEK CELL — with rows for shape, cell wall visible (yes/no), nucleus visible (yes/no), colour, other observations.
framework.teacherMoves: Teacher first demonstrates the onion peel preparation at the front bench, narrating each step: 'I am peeling the inner layer — it must be thin enough to be almost transparent. If it is too thick the light will not pass through and you will see nothing. Watch how I lower the cover slip at an angle to push out air bubbles.' Teacher then circulates during the practical, asking targeted questions at each bench: 'What is the shape of the onion cells — does it remind you of anything you have seen in Kenya? Think of the bricks used to build a nyumba.' At the cheek cell stage, teacher reminds the class firmly: 'Your cotton bud goes in YOUR mouth. No sharing. After use, it goes directly into the Jik beaker — not on the bench, not on the floor.' Teacher uses a document camera or pre-prepared large diagram to show learners the difference between a focused cell and an air bubble so they can self-correct. If microscopes are limited, teacher ensures rotation so every learner has at least 5 minutes of independent viewing time. Teacher says: 'When you are looking through the eyepiece, I want you to ask yourself three questions: What shape do I see? Can I see a boundary around each cell? Can I see anything inside? You have 12 minutes. Observe first, draw second — do not start drawing until you have spent at least 2 minutes just looking.' [WAIT TIME: Teacher pauses after circulating to the third bench and asks the whole class to freeze and share one surprising observation — 30 seconds of whole-class listening before continuing practical work.]
framework.sensemakingStrategy: The structured comparison table forces learners to observe with purpose rather than passively looking. The use of iodine (which stains starch and makes cell walls and nuclei more visible in onion cells) and methylene blue (which stains nuclei in cheek cells) makes abstract structures visible, connecting the chemistry of staining to the biology of observation. The brick analogy for the rectangular onion cells connects to a familiar Kenyan construction context, making the plant cell wall concept tangible before it is formally named.
framework.formativeAssessment: Teacher examines biological drawings during circulation. Key criteria checked informally: (1) Is the drawing in pencil and occupying most of the circle? (2) Are label lines ruled and touching the structure — not arrows? (3) Is the magnification recorded? (4) Are at least two structures labelled per cell type? Teacher identifies one strong drawing from each cell type to display on the document camera during the Explain Phase debrief. Comparison tables are scanned for the key productive observation: most learners will note that the onion cell has a defined rectangular wall and that the cheek cell does NOT — this is the central evidence piece for the lesson.

framework.learnerExperience: Learners close their microscopes and return to their seats. The teacher displays two reference biological drawings on the board — one of an onion epidermal cell and one of a cheek cell, both correctly labelled with: cell wall, cell membrane, cytoplasm, nucleus, and vacuole (onion only). Learners compare these to their own drawings and self-assess using a 3-point rubric written on the board: (1) I drew the correct shape and size relative to the field of view; (2) I labelled at least 3 structures with ruled lines; (3) I recorded the magnification. Learners circle where they scored well and underline one thing to improve. The teacher then leads a whole-class discussion focused on the comparison table: 'What were the biggest differences you recorded between your onion cell and your cheek cell?' Learners share and teacher builds a consensus comparison on the board. Teacher then returns to the Lesson 1 board predictions: 'Which predictions were correct? Which surprised you?' Key surprises are highlighted — especially the absence of green colour in the onion cell (expected by some learners who confused onion with sukuma wiki) and the difficulty of seeing the nucleus clearly in cheek cells. Teacher introduces the formal names for structures observed: cell wall, cell membrane, nucleus, cytoplasm, vacuole. Teacher clarifies: 'Today you observed structures. We have NOT yet explained what each structure DOES. That is the question we carry into Lesson 3 and Lesson 4.'
framework.teacherMoves: Teacher projects the two reference drawings and says: 'Compare your drawing to this. I am not looking for perfection — I am looking for honesty. Where did you get it right? Where do you need to improve? You have 90 seconds.' [WAIT TIME: 90 seconds of silent self-assessment.] Teacher then cold-calls 4 learners to share one difference they recorded in their comparison table. Teacher uses these contributions to build the consensus table on the board, saying: 'Kweli — that is genuine scientific evidence. You observed that the onion cell has a thick boundary that the cheek cell does NOT have. We are going to call that the cell wall. Write that term next to your observation in your table.' Teacher asks: 'Did anyone predict they would see green structures in the onion cell?' [Hands raised.] 'And did you see green?' [No.] 'Interesting. So the sukuma wiki cell we saw in Lesson 1 had green structures, but the onion cell does not — even though both are plants. What question does that raise for you?' Teacher pauses and gestures toward the DQB. [WAIT TIME: 20 seconds for learners to formulate a question mentally before the DQB phase begins.]
framework.sensemakingStrategy: Self-assessment against the reference drawing develops metacognitive awareness of scientific drawing conventions. Returning to Lesson 1 predictions closes the predict-observe loop and makes the cognitive dissonance of surprising results explicit and productive. Naming structures only AFTER observation ensures that vocabulary is anchored to direct experience rather than introduced abstractly — this is a key constructivist move in the storyline.
framework.formativeAssessment: The self-assessment rubric gives the teacher rapid data on drawing quality without marking every book. Teacher specifically looks for learners who labelled the cell wall on the cheek cell (a misconception — cheek cells have a membrane, not a rigid wall) and addresses this immediately: 'Remember — you did NOT see a thick rectangular wall around your cheek cell. Only the onion cell had that. Correct your label if needed.' This corrects a high-frequency misconception before it solidifies.

framework.learnerExperience: Each learner receives one sticky note (or tears a small piece of paper if sticky notes are unavailable). They write ONE question that arose from today's practical — something they observed but cannot yet explain. Learners are prompted with sentence starters on the board: 'I noticed ______ and I wonder why ______.' or 'I expected to see ______ but instead I saw ______ — why?' Learners post their question notes on the class Driving Question Board under the section labelled LESSON 2: MICROSCOPY EVIDENCE. Teacher reads 5 to 6 questions aloud, clusters related ones together with a marker circle, and identifies which questions will be answered in Lessons 3 and 4 versus which will need to wait for later in the unit. Examples of expected learner questions: 'Why did the onion cell not have green colour but the sukuma wiki cell does?', 'What is the jelly-like stuff inside the cheek cell?', 'Why could I not see a nucleus clearly in my cheek cell?', 'Why is the onion cell shaped like a rectangle but the cheek cell is round?', 'Is the thick wall around the onion cell the same as the cell membrane?'
framework.teacherMoves: Teacher says: 'Scientists do not only record what they see — they record what confused them. That confusion is the engine of science. Write your most burning question from today on your piece of paper. One question only — your most important one. You have 60 seconds.' [WAIT TIME: 60 seconds of independent writing, no discussion.] As learners post their questions, teacher reads them quickly. Teacher then selects questions and says: 'This question — why did the onion cell not have green colour — is connected to our original phenomenon. Remember the sukuma wiki leaf from Lesson 1? We are going to answer this in Lesson 4 when we study plant-specific organelles. I am writing L4 next to this cluster. This question — what is the jelly inside the cheek cell — we will begin to answer in Lesson 3. I am writing L3 next to this cluster.' Teacher says: 'The DQB is now our research agenda. Every lesson from here, we come back to it and answer more questions. By Lesson 8, the goal is to answer the driving question completely — using evidence.'
framework.sensemakingStrategy: The DQB technique transforms student confusion into a visible, shared research agenda, making the inquiry process transparent and collaborative. Clustering questions by lesson anticipates the storyline structure, helping learners see that the unit is a coherent investigation rather than a series of disconnected topics. This move also validates the cognitive dissonance learners experienced during the practical as productive scientific thinking.
framework.formativeAssessment: The DQB questions serve as a window into learner thinking. Teacher notes: (1) Are learners asking questions about structures (observable) or about function (explanatory)? The latter indicates deeper thinking. (2) Are any questions revealing persistent misconceptions from Lesson 1 that were not corrected? These are flagged for follow-up in Lesson 3. Teacher photographs the DQB at the end of class for reference.

framework.learnerExperience: In the final 5 minutes, learners do a quick individual task: they update their Lesson 1 basic cell sketch using today's observational evidence. They must (a) add or correct at least two structures based on what they actually saw today, (b) write the name of the structure next to each correction, and (c) use a different colour or symbol to mark structures they OBSERVED today versus structures they only predicted in Lesson 1. This updated sketch becomes Version 2 of their evolving cell model, which will be progressively refined through Lessons 3 to 7 until the final 3D model construction in Lesson 7. Learners write the date and the label VERSION 2 — BASED ON MICROSCOPY EVIDENCE at the top of the updated sketch.
framework.teacherMoves: Teacher says: 'Your Lesson 1 sketch was your first model — built on prediction alone. Now you have evidence. Scientists revise their models when they get new evidence. That is not weakness — that is science. In the next 4 minutes, update your sketch. Use today as evidence. If you predicted something in Lesson 1 that you did NOT see today, you do not cross it out — you write a question mark next to it, because we may yet find it in a different type of cell.' Teacher circulates and prompts learners who are uncertain: 'What did you actually see in the onion cell that you did not draw in Lesson 1? Start with the shape.' [WAIT TIME: Teacher gives 3 full minutes of independent work before making any whole-class comments.] With 1 minute remaining, teacher says: 'Your model will keep changing. By Lesson 7 you will build a 3D version using materials from around your home. Keep every version — they are evidence of your own thinking growing.'
framework.sensemakingStrategy: The iterative model-building strategy makes the nature of science explicit: models are provisional and evidence-driven. Version-labelling creates a longitudinal record of conceptual change that learners can trace across the unit, supporting metacognition. The instruction to mark observed versus predicted structures forces learners to distinguish between empirical evidence and inference — a core science practice.
framework.formativeAssessment: Teacher collects 5 to 6 updated sketches at the end of class (rotating so that all learners submit once across the unit). Teacher checks: Did the learner add the cell wall to the plant cell and omit it from the animal cell sketch? Is the nucleus now drawn in both cell types but noted as difficult to see in the cheek cell? Are any organelles (such as mitochondria) drawn that were NOT visible today — and if so, does the learner mark them with a question mark, indicating they know the limits of their current evidence? These questions guide the teacher opening of Lesson 3.

LESSONS.teacherReflection: After the lesson, consider the following questions: (1) Were all learners able to achieve a focused image of at least one cell type? If not, what microscope management adjustment is needed — rotation schedule, demonstration frequency, or peer-support pairing? (2) Which misconceptions appeared most frequently in comparison tables — for example, labelling the cell membrane as the cell wall in cheek cells, or expecting chloroplasts in the onion? Plan a 2-minute correction opener for Lesson 3. (3) What questions on the DQB reveal the richest thinking? Prioritise these in Lesson 3 planning. (4) Did the practical take longer than allocated? If so, consider whether Part B (cheek cells) can be done as a demonstration in future, freeing time for deeper discussion. (5) Were safety protocols followed by all learners, particularly the cotton bud discard procedure? If not, revisit the safety briefing explicitly at the start of Lesson 3.

summaryTablePrompt.observed: Onion epidermal cells are rectangular, have a thick outer wall, and show a visible vacuole when stained with iodine. Cheek cells are irregular and rounder in shape, do not have a thick outer wall, and show a faint nucleus when stained with methylene blue. The onion cell did NOT show the green colour seen in the sukuma wiki leaf cell from Lesson 1.
summaryTablePrompt.learned: Both plant and animal cells share a cell membrane and cytoplasm. Plant cells (onion) also have a cell wall that gives them a rigid rectangular shape, and a large vacuole. Animal cells (cheek) lack a cell wall and have a more irregular shape. Structures visible under a light microscope include the cell wall, nucleus, and vacuole — but many internal structures are too small to see at this magnification.
summaryTablePrompt.explained: The rectangular shape of the onion cell is explained by the presence of a rigid cell wall — a structure the cheek cell does not have. The absence of green colour in the onion cell (even though it is a plant cell) raises a new question that will be explained in Lesson 4 when students study chloroplasts and where in a plant they are found.

LESSONS.title: What Is Inside the Cell Doing All the Work? — Organelle Structure & Function (Animal Cell)

slo.purpose: To systematically identify animal cell organelles from electron micrograph images and annotated diagrams, and explain how each organelle's structure enables its specific function.
slo.knowledge: Learners identify and describe the structure and function of animal cell organelles: nucleus, cell membrane, cytoplasm, mitochondria, ribosomes, rough and smooth endoplasmic reticulum, Golgi apparatus, lysosomes, and centrioles.
slo.skills: Learners interpret electron micrograph images, annotate biological diagrams, construct factory-analogy concept maps, and use evidence from the anchoring phenomenon to justify organelle-function relationships.
slo.attitudes: Learners appreciate the remarkable precision and interdependence of cellular structures, and develop curiosity about how microscopic organisation underpins the visible differences observed in the blood smear and sukuma wiki slide from the anchoring phenomenon.
slo.keyInquiry: What are the structural differences between plant and animal cells? How does cell specialisation enable different cells to perform specific functions in living organisms?
slo.purposeInStoryline: In Lessons 1–2 learners observed the anchoring phenomenon (the blood smear and sukuma wiki leaf slide) and generated DQB questions about WHY cells look different. In Lesson 3, learners now open the 'black box' of the animal cell — they examine what organelles exist inside, what each one does, and begin to build a reference model of a generic animal cell. This prepares them to explain in Lessons 4–5 why specialised cells (e.g., the enucleated red blood cell, the white blood cell with a multi-lobed nucleus) deviate from this generic model.
slo.safetyNotes: No hazardous materials are used in this lesson. If printed electron micrograph images are handled, ensure hands are clean. Remind learners to handle printed materials carefully. If the school has a projector, ensure cables are secured to avoid tripping hazards.

LESSONS.overview: Learners begin by revisiting the anchoring phenomenon — the blood smear image showing enucleated red blood cells beside nucleated white blood cells — and reactivate their DQB questions from Lessons 1–2. They predict which organelles must be inside a 'typical' animal cell before any instruction. They then systematically observe a set of annotated electron micrograph images (or high-quality printed diagrams) of animal cell organelles, matching each to its structure and function. The central sensemaking activity uses a Nairobi Industrial Park factory analogy: the cell is a manufacturing factory in the Industrial Area along Mombasa Road, and each organelle maps onto a department in that factory (nucleus = Managing Director's office; mitochondria = generator room; ribosomes = production line workers; rough ER = internal transport corridor; Golgi apparatus = packaging and dispatch department; lysosomes = waste management and security unit; centrioles = site engineers who set up scaffolding during construction). Learners build an annotated concept map linking organelle → structure feature → function → factory-analogy role → connection to the phenomenon. The lesson closes with a return to the DQB where learners post 'answered' sticky notes and add new refined questions, advancing the driving question.

framework.learnerExperience: Learners are shown the anchoring phenomenon image on the board (or printed handout): the blood smear showing biconcave red blood cells with NO nucleus alongside white blood cells WITH large, visible nuclei. The teacher poses the predict prompt: 'Before we open up a typical animal cell today — write down in your notebook: What organelles do you think must be inside a working animal cell? Sketch a rough outline and label at least 4 structures you believe are there, and write ONE reason why you think each one must exist.' Learners work individually and silently for 5 minutes, then share with a shoulder partner for 2 minutes. The teacher cold-calls 4 different learners to share one prediction each and records responses on the board under the heading 'Our Predictions — Lesson 3'. Learners are explicitly told: 'We will come back to this board at the end of the lesson and tick off what we got right — and explain what surprised us.'
framework.teacherMoves:
1. Display or distribute the anchoring phenomenon image. Say verbatim: 'Look again at our blood smear from Kisumu County Hospital. You told me in Lessons 1 and 2 that the red blood cell has NO nucleus. But what about the white blood cell sitting right next to it — it clearly has structures inside. Today we are going to find out exactly WHAT is inside a typical animal cell.' 
2. Read the predict prompt aloud and write it on the board. Give learners 5 minutes of silent individual thinking — enforce this silence. 
3. After 5 minutes, say: 'Turn to your shoulder partner. Share your sketch and your 4 organelles. You have 2 minutes.' 
4. After partner talk, cold-call EXACTLY 4 learners (select different rows/gender): 'Akinyi, tell me ONE organelle you predicted and why.' Record each on the board. WAIT 10–12 seconds after each response before probing. 
5. Say: 'Hold onto these predictions — we are scientists today. At the end of the lesson, we will return to this board and evaluate our thinking.'
framework.sensemakingStrategy: Elicit-then-Compare Prediction Routine — By committing predictions to paper before instruction, learners activate prior knowledge and create a cognitive 'anchor point'. Recording predictions publicly on the board creates accountability and a reference point for reflection at the lesson's end, deepening metacognitive awareness of what was learned.
framework.formativeAssessment: Observable indicator: Each learner has a sketch with at least 2–4 labelled structures in their notebook before partner talk begins. The teacher scans the room during silent work and notes whether learners are drawing anything at all (engagement check) and whether any learner already uses correct terminology (e.g., 'nucleus', 'mitochondria') — this informs pacing. During cold-calling, the teacher listens for reasoning quality: are learners giving structure-function reasons ('I think there must be something that gives energy because cells do work') rather than purely memorised labels?

framework.learnerExperience: Learners receive a set of 4 printed A4 resource sheets (or view on screen if projector is available): Sheet 1 — a labelled transmission electron micrograph (TEM) of a generalised animal cell with key organelles visible; Sheet 2 — close-up electron micrograph images of individual organelles (nucleus showing nuclear envelope and nucleolus; mitochondria showing cristae; rough ER showing ribosomes studded on membrane; Golgi apparatus showing stacked cisternae; lysosome; centriole pair); Sheet 3 — a blank animal cell outline diagram for annotation; Sheet 4 — an Organelle Evidence Table with columns: Organelle Name | What I can SEE in the image (structural features) | What I THINK it does (from image alone). In pairs, learners examine each electron micrograph image on Sheet 2, record at least 2 observable structural features for each organelle in their Evidence Table (Sheet 4), and make an inference about its function based purely on what they see. They do NOT receive function information yet — they must reason from the images.
framework.teacherMoves:
1. Distribute resource sheets. Say verbatim: 'You are now going to do what a real cell biologist does — look at images and describe EXACTLY what you see. Do NOT jump to telling me what you know from memory. Only write what the image shows you.' 
2. Model one example with the nucleus: project or hold up the nucleus micrograph. Say: 'I can see a large, dark, roughly spherical structure. It appears to be enclosed by a double membrane. There is a darker dense region inside — I do not yet know what that is. I am writing exactly that.' 
3. Circulate the room. At EACH group, ask at least one probing question: 'What does the wavy folded membrane inside the mitochondria SUGGEST to you about its function?' OR 'Why do you think the ribosomes are so TINY compared to the nucleus?' WAIT 10–12 seconds after posing each question before accepting a response. 
4. After 15 minutes of pair work, cold-call 5 learners (different from Phase 1) to share one structural observation each for 5 different organelles. Say: 'Otieno, what did you SEE — not what you know — for the mitochondria?' Record on the board. 
5. Affirm pattern-noticing: 'Notice how the structure always gives us a clue about the job. We will use that principle all lesson.'
framework.sensemakingStrategy: Image-Based Structural Inference (Science & Engineering Practice: Analysing and Interpreting Data) — Learners extract evidence directly from electron micrograph images before receiving any expository information. This mirrors the practice of real cell biologists and ensures learners engage with visual scientific data rather than passively receiving facts. The Evidence Table scaffolds disciplined observation (SEE) distinct from interpretation (THINK), building epistemological rigour.
framework.formativeAssessment: Observable indicator: Completed Evidence Table rows for at least 5 of 7 organelles with distinct structural observations (not just 'it is round'). The teacher checks specifically for quality of structural language — are learners describing membranes, shapes, internal features? A learner who writes 'the mitochondria has folded inner membranes that increase surface area' is demonstrating higher-order inference. Flag any pairs who are writing only one-word observations for immediate scaffolding support during circulation.

framework.learnerExperience: The teacher introduces the Nairobi Industrial Park Factory Analogy. Learners are told: 'Imagine a large manufacturing factory in Nairobi's Industrial Area along Mombasa Road — the kind that assembles products, packages them, ships them, and keeps the lights on. This factory IS your animal cell.' The teacher reveals a large analogy mapping poster (or board diagram) one organelle at a time. For each organelle, the teacher provides the structure-function explanation and then asks learners to articulate the factory-department connection themselves before confirming it. The full analogy set is: (1) Nucleus = Managing Director's Office — holds the master blueprint (DNA), sends instructions to all departments; (2) Cell Membrane = Security gate and perimeter wall — controls what enters and leaves the factory; (3) Cytoplasm = Factory floor — the space where all work happens; (4) Mitochondria = Generator room — converts fuel (glucose) into usable electrical energy (ATP); (5) Ribosomes = Production line workers on the factory floor — assemble products (proteins) following the MD's instructions; (6) Rough ER = Internal conveyor corridor with workers attached — transports newly made proteins and modifies them; (7) Smooth ER = Separate chemical processing unit — produces lipids and detoxifies substances; (8) Golgi Apparatus = Packaging and dispatch department — receives, labels, packages, and ships products (proteins/lipids) to correct destinations; (9) Lysosomes = Waste management and security unit — breaks down faulty products, waste, and invading materials using enzymes; (10) Centrioles = Site engineers/scaffold builders — organise the cell's internal skeleton during cell division. After the full analogy is built, learners annotate their blank cell diagram (Sheet 3) with organelle names, one structural feature, one function, and their factory role. Learners then individually answer: 'Using the factory analogy, explain WHY a red blood cell from the Kisumu blood smear has NO nucleus — what department is missing, and what does that mean for the cell's capabilities?'
framework.teacherMoves:
1. Introduce the analogy: 'I am going to describe a factory in Nairobi's Industrial Area. Your job is to tell me which organelle matches each department.' Reveal organelles ONE at a time — do NOT show the full list at once. 
2. For each organelle: (a) State the structural feature from the electron micrograph. (b) Ask learners to predict the factory match BEFORE you confirm. Say: 'What department does THIS remind you of?' WAIT 12 seconds. Cold-call one learner. (c) Confirm or redirect, then give the full function explanation. 
3. After all 10 organelles, say: 'Now annotate your blank diagram — Sheet 3. You have 8 minutes. Each organelle needs: name, one structural feature, one function, factory role.' Circulate and check annotations. 
4. Then pose the phenomenon-connection question verbatim: 'Using what you now know about organelles — particularly the nucleus — explain WHY the red blood cell in our Kisumu blood smear has NO nucleus. What does losing that organelle mean for the cell? Write 3 sentences in your notebook.' WAIT for all learners to begin writing before circulating. 
5. Cold-call 3 learners to share their explanations. Probe: 'If the nucleus is the MD's office, and the red blood cell has no MD's office — what CAN'T that cell do that the white blood cell CAN?' WAIT 10 seconds. Affirm reasoning that connects to DNA, protein synthesis, or cell reproduction.
framework.sensemakingStrategy: Anchored Analogy Mapping (Science & Engineering Practice: Constructing Explanations) — The Nairobi Industrial Park analogy provides a culturally grounded, concrete schema that learners can map onto abstract organelle functions. Critically, learners are asked to generate the mapping connection themselves before confirmation, making the analogy an active reasoning tool rather than a passive mnemonic. The return to the anchoring phenomenon (enucleated red blood cell) forces learners to apply new knowledge to authentic evidence, consolidating understanding through explanation rather than recall.
framework.formativeAssessment: Observable indicator 1: Annotated blank diagram (Sheet 3) has all 10 organelles labelled with function and factory role — teacher checks for accuracy during circulation, particularly whether learners correctly distinguish rough ER (ribosomes attached, protein transport) from smooth ER (no ribosomes, lipid synthesis). Observable indicator 2: The 3-sentence written explanation about the enucleated red blood cell. A strong response connects nucleus absence → no DNA → cannot produce proteins or replicate → cell optimised purely for oxygen transport with maximum space for haemoglobin. A weak response merely states 'it has no nucleus' without functional consequences — teacher uses this to identify learners requiring Tier 2 support.

framework.learnerExperience: Learners return to the class Driving Question Board, which has been displayed since Lesson 1. Each learner has two sticky note colours: GREEN = 'I can now answer this question' and ORANGE = 'I have a NEW question from today's lesson.' Learners spend 4 minutes independently reviewing the DQB, selecting at least ONE question they can now partially or fully answer (green sticky note with their written answer summary), and writing at least ONE new question prompted by today's organelle learning (orange sticky note).
