New questions may include: 'If the sukuma wiki leaf cell also has a nucleus — does it have the SAME type of nucleus as the white blood cell?' or 'Do plant cells have mitochondria too?' or 'Why do some cells have MORE mitochondria than others?' Learners then do a gallery walk of 3 minutes around the DQB to read peers' new orange questions, placing a small dot sticker next to any new question they also want answered. The teacher tallies the top 3 most-dotted new questions and announces: 'These three questions will DRIVE our next two lessons.'
framework.teacherMoves:
1. Direct learners to the DQB: 'Take 4 minutes — quietly and independently. Find ONE question on our board you can now answer because of today's lesson. Write your answer summary on a GREEN sticky note and place it over or beside that question.' 
2. Then: 'Now write at least ONE new question today's lesson sparked for you — on an ORANGE sticky note. This must be a genuine question, not one you already know the answer to.' 
3. After posting, announce the 3-minute gallery walk: 'Walk around the board. Read your classmates' new orange questions. Place a dot sticker next to any question YOU also want answered.' 
4. After the gallery walk, tally the top 3 most-dotted questions aloud: 'The class most wants to know: [read top 3]. These will anchor Lessons 4 and 5.' 
5. Say verbatim: 'Notice how our DQB is growing — not just with answers, but with better, more precise questions. That is what scientists do. The more you know, the better your questions become.'
framework.sensemakingStrategy: Driving Question Board Revision (Science & Engineering Practice: Asking Questions and Defining Problems) — The DQB is a living document that makes the progression of learner understanding visible over the unit. Using two sticky note colours distinguishes consolidation (answered questions) from extension (new questions), preventing the misconception that learning means 'having all the answers'. The dot-voting gallery walk democratises the inquiry process and gives the teacher real-time formative data on learner curiosity and conceptual gaps.
framework.formativeAssessment: Observable indicator: Every learner has posted at least one green and one orange sticky note. The teacher photographs the DQB after the lesson for records. The teacher reads green sticky note answer summaries after class to assess accuracy of self-assessed understanding — particularly whether learners correctly connect organelle function to the phenomenon (e.g., 'The red blood cell has no nucleus = no Managing Director = cannot make new proteins or divide'). Orange questions that show surface thinking ('What is a Golgi?') versus deep thinking ('If the Golgi packages proteins, what happens in a cell where the Golgi is damaged?') are noted for differentiation planning.

framework.learnerExperience: Learners open their running 'Cell Model Journal' — a dedicated section of their notebook begun in Lesson 1 — and locate their Lesson 1/2 sketched model of 'what we think a cell looks like'. Using a different coloured pen (to show revision), learners now update their model to reflect today's learning. They must: (1) Add and label all 10 animal cell organelles with structural annotations; (2) Draw arrows showing the functional pathway of protein synthesis — from nucleus (DNA instruction) → ribosome (assembly) → rough ER (transport) → Golgi (packaging) → cell membrane (export) — labelling each arrow with the factory analogy role; (3) In a text box beside the model, write: 'How is this GENERIC animal cell model different from the RED BLOOD CELL we saw in the Kisumu blood smear? List at least 2 structural differences and explain what each difference means for function.' Learners will continue building this same model in Lessons 4–8 as new evidence accumulates.
framework.teacherMoves:
1. Instruct learners to open their Cell Model Journal to their Lesson 1 sketch. Say: 'Scientists revise their models when they get new evidence. Today you have new evidence — use a different-coloured pen to show that this is a REVISION, not a replacement.' 
2. Write the three model tasks on the board for reference. Give learners 7 minutes for model revision. Circulate and check specifically: (a) Is the protein synthesis pathway arrow sequence correct? (b) Does the comparison text box include FUNCTIONAL consequences, not just structural observations? 
3. After 7 minutes, cold-call 2 learners to share one thing they revised in their model and WHY. Ask: 'What did your Lesson 1 model get wrong or miss? How do you know?' WAIT 10 seconds. 
4. Close with the driving question: 'Our model today is of a GENERIC animal cell. But we know from our blood smear that the red blood cell is NOT a generic cell — it has been modified. Next lesson, we will find out what a plant cell looks like and then start comparing ALL of these to our generic model to explain the original puzzle.' 
5. Collect or photograph 3–4 Cell Model Journals for formative review before Lesson 4.
framework.sensemakingStrategy: Cumulative Model Revision (Science & Engineering Practice: Developing and Using Models) — The Cell Model Journal functions as a longitudinal scientific model that accumulates evidence across all 8 lessons. Using a different-coloured pen for each revision makes the history of thinking visible — both to the learner (metacognitive) and to the teacher (formative). Drawing the protein synthesis pathway as a functional arrow sequence moves learners from listing organelles to understanding organelles as an integrated, interdependent system.
framework.formativeAssessment: Observable indicator: Revised model contains all 10 organelles AND the protein synthesis arrow pathway with labels. The comparison text box must include at minimum: (1) Red blood cell has no nucleus — cannot make proteins or divide; (2) Red blood cell has no mitochondria — relies on anaerobic processes; must include haemoglobin as functional adaptation. Teacher uses the 3–4 collected journals to identify: (a) Learners with accurate, detailed models (ready for extension in Lesson 4); (b) Learners whose pathway arrows are in wrong order — reteach sequence in Lesson 4 warm-up; (c) Learners who cannot articulate functional consequences of structural differences — provide Tier 2 support via paired discussion at start of Lesson 4.

LESSONS.teacherReflection: After the lesson, reflect on the following: (1) PREDICTION QUALITY: Were learners' Phase 1 predictions richer than expected, suggesting stronger prior knowledge from Lessons 1–2? Or were most predictions vague (e.g., 'a nucleus and some other stuff'), suggesting the earlier lessons need more scaffolding of organelle vocabulary? (2) ANALOGY ENGAGEMENT: Did the Nairobi Industrial Park factory analogy land — did learners enthusiastically map organelles to departments, or did some learners find the analogy forced or confusing? If learners proposed their own alternative Kenyan analogies (e.g., a matatu sacco, a market stall), note these for use in Lesson 4. (3) PHENOMENON CONNECTION: When asked to explain WHY the red blood cell lacks a nucleus, how many learners went beyond description ('it doesn't have one') to functional consequence ('it cannot synthesise proteins or replicate')? If fewer than 60% achieved the functional explanation, plan a 5-minute reteach warm-up at the start of Lesson 4 using the matatu analogy: 'A matatu with no driver (nucleus) can carry passengers (haemoglobin/oxygen) but cannot navigate new routes or reproduce.' (4) DQB QUALITY: Were the new orange questions showing genuine conceptual depth (e.g., 'If plant cells also have a nucleus — do they have the same organelles as animal cells?') — these signal readiness to advance to Lessons 4–5. Surface questions suggest learners need more processing time. (5) MODEL REVISION: Check collected Cell Model Journals before Lesson 4. Flag learners whose protein synthesis pathways are incomplete or disordered — the sequence nucleus → ribosome → rough ER → Golgi → cell membrane is a threshold concept for all subsequent lessons on secretion and transport in Strands 2 and 3.

summaryTablePrompt.observed: Learners examined annotated electron micrograph images and close-up TEM images of individual animal cell organelles (nucleus, mitochondria, ribosomes, rough and smooth ER, Golgi apparatus, lysosomes, centrioles), recording structural features they could directly see in an Organelle Evidence Table before receiving any expository function information.
summaryTablePrompt.learned: Learners identified and explained the structure and function of 10 animal cell organelles, mapping each to a department in a Nairobi Industrial Park factory analogy. Learners traced the protein synthesis functional pathway (nucleus → ribosome → rough ER → Golgi → cell membrane) and connected organelle knowledge back to the anchoring phenomenon by explaining why the Kisumu blood smear red blood cell lacks a nucleus and what functional consequences follow.
summaryTablePrompt.explained: Learners explained that each organelle's structure is directly related to its function — for example, the folded inner membranes (cristae) of the mitochondria maximise surface area for ATP production, just as a generator room is sized to power the whole factory. They explained the anchoring phenomenon observation — the enucleated red blood cell — as a specialisation trade-off: losing the nucleus (Managing Director's office) sacrifices the ability to make proteins or replicate, but maximises internal space for haemoglobin and oxygen transport, a function critical to its role in the bloodstream.

LESSONS.aresKeywords: animal cell organelles, nucleus function, mitochondria, ribosomes, endoplasmic reticulum, Golgi apparatus, lysosomes, centrioles, electron micrograph, cell biology Grade 10, Kenya CBC Biology, organelle function, factory analogy, protein synthesis pathway, cell specialisation, enucleated red blood cell, anchoring phenomenon, Nairobi Industrial Park analogy, cell model revision, DQB, driving question board

LESSONS.title: Organelle Structure & Function — The Plant Cell: How Is a Sukuma Wiki Cell Like and Unlike a Human Cell?

slo.purpose: To explain how plant-specific organelles — the chloroplast, cell wall, and central vacuole — give plant cells their unique structure and enable them to perform functions that animal cells cannot.
slo.knowledge: By the end of this lesson, the learner should be able to: (a) identify and describe the structure of plant-specific organelles (chloroplast, cell wall, central vacuole, plasmodesmata); (b) explain how each plant-specific organelle relates to a specific function (photosynthesis, structural rigidity, turgor pressure); (c) compare and contrast the structures of a generalised plant cell and a generalised animal cell using a structured comparison table.
slo.skills: By the end of this lesson, the learner should be able to: (a) construct an accurate, labelled 2D diagram of a plant cell and an animal cell; (b) use evidence from the sukuma wiki leaf observation (Lesson 3) and the blood smear phenomenon (Lesson 1) to justify structural differences between plant and animal cells; (c) apply understanding of turgor pressure to predict what happens to a bean plant cell when water is scarce.
slo.attitudes: By the end of this lesson, the learner should be able to: (a) appreciate how microscopic structural differences in cells explain everyday observable phenomena such as wilting crops in a Kenyan shamba; (b) demonstrate curiosity and collaboration while constructing and peer-reviewing 2D cell diagrams; (c) value the importance of evidence-based reasoning when comparing cell types.
slo.keyInquiry: 1. What are the structural differences between plant and animal cells? 2. How does cell specialisation enable different cells to perform specific functions in living organisms?
slo.purposeInStoryline: In Lessons 1–3, students gathered evidence about the blood smear phenomenon (RBCs, WBCs) and observed sukuma wiki leaf cells under a microscope, noticing the green structures, thick borders, and a large central sac. In Lesson 4, students now EXPLAIN those observations: they learn the names and functions of plant-specific organelles and use that knowledge to begin building a comparative 2D model of plant vs. animal cells — a direct step toward answering the driving question about why all these structures are called 'cells' yet look so different.
slo.safetyNotes: No chemicals or sharp instruments are used in this lesson. If using wilted vs. turgid bean seedlings as a demonstration, ensure seedlings are handled gently. Wash hands after handling plant material. Ensure diagrams are drawn in pencil first before inking, to allow correction without waste.

LESSONS.overview: This 80-minute EXPLAIN-phase lesson helps Grade 10 learners make sense of their Lesson 3 observations of sukuma wiki leaf cells. Students revisit their Lesson 3 observation drawings and use guided direct instruction, a wilting bean seedling demonstration, a structured comparison table, and collaborative 2D model-building to explain the structure and function of the three plant-specific organelles (chloroplast, cell wall, central vacuole). The lesson culminates in students beginning their labelled 2D comparative cell diagrams — a cumulative model they will refine across subsequent lessons. All explanations are anchored to the driving question phenomenon: why do blood cells and sukuma wiki leaf cells look so completely different, yet both qualify as 'cells'?

framework.learnerExperience: Students open their science notebooks to their Lesson 3 observation drawings of the sukuma wiki leaf cells. Working individually for 3 minutes, they annotate their drawings by circling THREE structures they noticed that they did NOT see in the blood smear from Lesson 1 (the anchoring phenomenon). They write one sentence predicting: 'I think the green structures in the sukuma wiki cell exist because...' and one sentence predicting: 'I think the thick border around the cell exists to...' Students then share their predictions in pairs (Think-Pair-Share, 2 minutes) before the teacher cold-calls 4 pairs to share with the class.
framework.teacherMoves: 1. Display the anchoring phenomenon split-screen: left side shows the blood smear image (RBCs and WBCs from Kisumu County Hospital); right side shows the sukuma wiki leaf cell image from Lesson 3. Say verbatim: 'Look at both images. You are the same student who donated blood AND who brought sukuma wiki from your shamba. Both slides are on the bench in front of you. What do you see in the leaf cell that is completely absent in your blood cell? Write it. Circle it. You have 3 minutes — go.' [Allow 3 minutes of silent individual work. WAIT TIME — do not speak.] 2. After 3 minutes, say: 'Now turn to your partner. Share your three circled structures and your two prediction sentences. You have 2 minutes.' [WAIT 2 minutes.] 3. Cold-call EXACTLY 4 pairs. For each pair, ask: 'What is ONE structure you circled, and what is your prediction for why it exists?' After each response, say: 'Interesting — hold that prediction. We are going to test it today.' Record student prediction keywords on the board under the heading: 'Our Predictions — We Will Explain These Today.' 4. Do NOT confirm or correct predictions at this stage. The goal is cognitive dissonance and curiosity.
framework.sensemakingStrategy: Think-Pair-Share with Annotated Drawing Review. This strategy activates students' own observation evidence from Lesson 3 as the starting point for new explanation, directly linking the OBSERVE phase to the EXPLAIN phase. By asking students to compare their leaf cell drawing to the blood smear phenomenon, the teacher creates productive cognitive conflict that motivates the explanation to follow.
framework.formativeAssessment: Observable indicator: Each student has at least 3 structures circled in their Lesson 3 drawing and has written 2 prediction sentences in their notebook. Teacher circulates during individual work and scans notebooks. If fewer than 60% of students can identify 3 distinct structures, teacher pauses and points to the projected image, naming one structure (e.g., 'What is this green oval shape?') to scaffold recall before proceeding.

framework.learnerExperience: The teacher displays two bean seedlings side by side: one well-watered (turgid, upright) and one that has been left without water for 48 hours (wilted, drooping). Students observe for 2 minutes and record in their notebooks: (a) one physical difference they can see with the naked eye, and (b) a prediction of what is happening INSIDE the cells to cause this difference. Students then watch a 3-minute annotated diagram walkthrough (teacher-drawn on the board OR a pre-prepared ARES platform diagram) showing the internal structure of a turgid vs. a wilted plant cell, focusing on the central vacuole. Students record key observations in a structured 'I Notice / I Wonder' table in their notebooks.
framework.teacherMoves: 1. Place the two bean seedlings (one turgid, one wilted) on the demonstration bench. Say verbatim: 'These two plants came from the same packet of beans. One is from a well-watered shamba in Kericho. One has not had water for two days — like a farm during a dry spell in Machakos. Observe them carefully. In your notebook, write ONE physical difference you can see, and predict what is happening INSIDE the cells. Two minutes — silent observation.' [WAIT 2 full minutes. Do not narrate. Let students observe.] 2. Cold-call 3 students. Ask each: 'What did you write for what is happening inside the cells?' After each response, say: 'We are going to use today's lesson to find out if you are right.' Write their predictions on the board. 3. Transition to the board diagram. Draw or display a labelled cross-section of a turgid plant cell and a wilted plant cell. Point to the central vacuole in each. Say: 'This large sac — the central vacuole — is full of water in the turgid cell and almost empty in the wilted cell. Remember the large water-filled sac you drew in your Lesson 3 sukuma wiki observation? THAT is this structure.' [Pause 10 seconds — WAIT TIME.] 4. Say: 'In your I Notice / I Wonder table, record: what you notice about the size of the central vacuole in each cell, and write at least one wonder question.' [Allow 3 minutes for recording.] 5. Cold-call 2 students to share their 'I Wonder' questions. Add these to the class Driving Question Board (DQB) parking lot.
framework.sensemakingStrategy: Concrete Phenomenon Observation + I Notice / I Wonder Table. Using real bean seedlings from a Kenyan farming context (Kericho / Machakos) grounds the abstract concept of turgor pressure in a visible, tangible, culturally relevant observation. The I Notice / I Wonder table is a structured sensemaking tool that separates empirical observation from inference, scaffolding the move from 'what I see' to 'what it means.'
framework.formativeAssessment: Observable indicator: Students' I Notice / I Wonder tables contain at least one accurate observational statement about the central vacuole size difference between turgid and wilted cells, and at least one genuine wonder question. Teacher scans 5–6 notebooks during the 3-minute recording period. Red flag: if students are writing only visual observations about the seedling (e.g., 'one is drooping') and NOT connecting to internal cell structure, teacher says: 'Look at the diagram on the board. What is INSIDE the cell that is different? Add that to your I Notice column.'

framework.learnerExperience: Students receive (or access on the ARES platform) a structured 'Plant Cell vs. Animal Cell Comparison Table' with columns: Organelle / Structure | Present in Plant Cell? | Present in Animal Cell? | Function | Connection to Our Phenomenon. The teacher delivers a focused 15-minute guided explanation of the three plant-specific organelles (chloroplast, cell wall, central vacuole), using the sukuma wiki leaf cell and the wilting bean as anchoring examples throughout. Students complete the comparison table collaboratively in pairs, pausing after each organelle for a 2-minute pair-fill. After the direct instruction, pairs share their completed rows with another pair (pair-to-pair check, 4 minutes). The class then reconstructs a consensus table on the board.
framework.teacherMoves: 1. Say: 'We are now going to NAME and EXPLAIN the three structures you circled in your Lesson 3 drawings. As I explain each one, you will fill in your comparison table WITH your partner. We pause after each organelle.' 2. CHLOROPLAST (5 min): Draw a chloroplast on the board. Say: 'This is the chloroplast — the green oval you saw in the sukuma wiki leaf cell. It contains a green pigment called chlorophyll. Its function is to capture energy from sunlight and use it to make food for the plant — a process called photosynthesis. This is why sukuma wiki is green. This is why a plant can make its OWN food while your blood cells cannot. Animal cells have NO chloroplasts.' Point to the phenomenon image: 'The student's blood cell — does it make its own food? No. It needs glucose delivered to it. The sukuma wiki leaf cell? It MAKES its own glucose. Same word — cell — completely different strategy.' [WAIT 10 seconds.] Say: 'Fill in the chloroplast row in your table now — 2 minutes with your partner.' [WAIT 2 minutes.] Cold-call 1 pair to read their 'Connection to Our Phenomenon' cell. 3. CELL WALL (5 min): Draw a cell wall on the board outside the cell membrane. Say: 'Every plant cell has a rigid outer layer called the cell wall, made of a tough substance called cellulose. It is like the fence around a shamba — it gives the cell its shape and strength. This is why the sukuma wiki cells you drew looked rectangular and the blood cells looked round and flexible. Animal cells have a cell membrane but NO cell wall.' Ask: 'Why might it be an advantage for a blood cell NOT to have a rigid wall?' [WAIT 15 seconds.] Cold-call 2 students. Expected answer: flexibility to squeeze through narrow capillaries. Say: 'Exactly — the RBC you saw in the blood smear needs to flex and bend. A rigid wall would be a disadvantage. Structure matches function.' [WAIT 10 seconds.] 'Fill in the cell wall row — 2 minutes.' 4. CENTRAL VACUOLE (5 min): Point back to the wilting bean diagram. Say: 'The large sac pushing everything to the side in your sukuma wiki drawing is the central vacuole. In a healthy plant cell, it is full of water and pushes the cytoplasm and organelles outward against the cell wall. This creates TURGOR PRESSURE — the internal pressure that keeps a plant upright and firm. When the central vacuole loses water — as in our wilted bean — turgor pressure drops, the cell becomes flaccid, and the plant droops. Animal cells have small vacuoles, but nothing like this.' Ask: 'If a sukuma wiki plant in your family's shamba in the Rift Valley wilts during a dry spell, what is happening at the cellular level?' [WAIT 15 seconds.] Cold-call 3 students. 'Fill in the central vacuole row — 2 minutes.' 5. Pair-to-Pair Check (4 min): Say: 'Turn to the pair next to you. Compare your tables row by row. If you disagree on any cell, discuss and decide together which answer is more accurate. Be ready to justify.' [WAIT 4 minutes.] 6. Cold-call 3 pairs to contribute one row each to the class consensus table on the board. Correct any misconceptions explicitly.
framework.sensemakingStrategy: Guided Direct Instruction with Pause-and-Process (Structured Comparison Table). The comparison table is a disciplinary sensemaking tool that makes abstract structural knowledge visible and comparative. By pausing after each organelle for a 2-minute pair-fill and connecting every explanation back to the anchoring phenomenon (blood smear vs. sukuma wiki cell), the teacher ensures students are not just receiving information but actively constructing meaning. The pair-to-pair check introduces peer accountability and surfaces misconceptions before whole-class consolidation.
framework.formativeAssessment: Observable indicator: Completed comparison table rows are accurate for all three plant-specific organelles. Specifically: (a) Chloroplast — students state 'photosynthesis / makes food using sunlight' as function and connect it to the sukuma wiki being green; (b) Cell wall — students state 'cellulose / rigidity / shape' and connect it to the rectangular appearance of leaf cells vs. round RBCs; (c) Central vacuole — students state 'stores water / turgor pressure / keeps plant upright' and connect it to the wilting bean. Red flag misconception to listen for: 'The cell wall IS the cell membrane' — address immediately: 'These are two separate structures. The cell membrane is present in ALL cells. The cell wall is an EXTRA layer found ONLY in plant cells, OUTSIDE the membrane.'

framework.learnerExperience: Students individually write one new sticky note (or DQB card on the ARES platform) in response to the prompt: 'What can I NOW explain about the driving question that I could NOT explain at the start of this lesson?' They also write one remaining 'burning question' — something today's lesson raised that they still cannot answer. Students post both cards on the class Driving Question Board under the headings 'NOW I CAN EXPLAIN' and 'STILL WONDERING.' The teacher reads aloud 4–5 cards from each column and uses them to preview upcoming lessons.
framework.teacherMoves: 1. Direct students to the DQB (physical board or ARES platform DQB feature). Say: 'You now know three things today that you did not know at the start of the lesson: what a chloroplast does, what a cell wall does, and what a central vacuole does. Write on your NOW I CAN EXPLAIN card — ONE sentence connecting today's learning to the driving question. For example: NOW I can explain why sukuma wiki cells look rectangular — they have a rigid cell wall made of cellulose. Be specific.' [Allow 3 minutes for writing.] 2. Say: 'Now write your STILL WONDERING card. What question did today's lesson CREATE for you that we have NOT yet answered? It must be a genuine question — not just a repeat of something I already explained.' [Allow 2 minutes.] 3. Students post cards. Teacher reads 4 NOW I CAN EXPLAIN cards aloud, affirming specificity. Then reads 3 STILL WONDERING cards aloud. For each STILL WONDERING card, say either: 'We will answer this in Lesson [X]' or 'This is a great question — let's add it to our board and return to it.' 4. Highlight the overarching driving question on the board. Say: 'We now understand the plant side of the puzzle. Next lesson, we revisit the animal cell — and then we begin to answer the BIG question: what do ALL cells share, despite looking so different?'
framework.sensemakingStrategy: Driving Question Board (DQB) — Now I Can Explain / Still Wondering Protocol. This metacognitive sensemaking strategy makes student learning growth visible and keeps the entire lesson sequence tethered to the driving question. The 'Still Wondering' cards are pedagogically vital: they surface genuine student curiosity and incomplete understanding, which the teacher can use to plan differentiation and sequence upcoming lessons responsively.
framework.formativeAssessment: Observable indicator: 'NOW I CAN EXPLAIN' cards contain a specific organelle name AND a functional explanation AND a connection to the phenomenon (blood smear or sukuma wiki cell). Vague cards such as 'I learned about plant cells' are redirected: teacher says, 'Can you name ONE organelle and say what job it does in the sukuma wiki cell?' Red flag: if the majority of 'STILL WONDERING' cards ask basic definitional questions (e.g., 'What is a chloroplast?'), this signals the direct instruction in Phase 3 was not retained and the teacher should open the next lesson with a brief retrieval practice activity before proceeding.

framework.learnerExperience: Students begin constructing their cumulative 2D labelled diagrams — one diagram of a generalised plant cell and one of a generalised animal cell — in their science notebooks. They use a provided scaffold (a blank outline of each cell type with leader lines for labels) available on the ARES platform. They must label: for the plant cell — cell wall, cell membrane, nucleus, cytoplasm, chloroplast, central vacuole, mitochondria; for the animal cell — cell membrane, nucleus, cytoplasm, mitochondria, small vacuoles. Students colour-code plant-specific organelles in green. In pairs, they peer-review each other's diagrams against a 4-point checklist: (1) All required organelles labelled, (2) Plant-specific organelles correctly identified, (3) Labels are accurate (no cell wall on animal cell, no chloroplast on animal cell), (4) Central vacuole is drawn large and central in the plant cell. Students sign their peer's diagram to confirm the review.
framework.teacherMoves: 1. Distribute or direct students to the ARES platform diagram scaffold. Say: 'You are now going to build your model — a labelled 2D diagram of BOTH cell types. This diagram will grow across the next four lessons as we add more detail. Today, use only what we have learned in Lessons 1–4. Draw in pencil first. Label clearly with leader lines. You have 12 minutes to complete both diagrams.' [Set a visible timer. Circulate continuously.] 2. During circulation, look for THREE specific errors: (a) students drawing a cell wall on the animal cell — correct immediately: 'Check your comparison table. Does an animal cell have a cell wall?'; (b) students drawing the central vacuole as small — say: 'In a turgid plant cell, the central vacuole occupies up to 90% of the cell volume. Make it BIG — it is pushing everything else to the edges'; (c) students omitting the cell membrane on the plant cell — say: 'The plant cell has BOTH a cell membrane AND a cell wall. The membrane is the thin layer just inside the wall. Add it.' 3. After 12 minutes, say: 'Pencils down. Exchange notebooks with your partner. Use the 4-point checklist on the board to review their diagrams. You have 5 minutes. If you find an error, circle it gently in pencil and write a question — do NOT just cross it out.' [WAIT 5 minutes.] 4. After peer review, say: 'Sign your partner's diagram to confirm you reviewed it. Then take back your own notebook and make any corrections.' [Allow 2 minutes for corrections.] 5. Close the model-building phase by saying: 'This diagram is Version 1 of your cell model. Every lesson from now, you will add to it. By Lesson 8, it will show every major organelle, every specialised cell type, and your full explanation of the driving question. Keep it safe.'
framework.sensemakingStrategy: Cumulative 2D Model Building with Structured Peer Review. Model-building is a core NGSS Science and Engineering Practice (Developing and Using Models). By making the diagrams explicitly cumulative ('Version 1'), the teacher signals that scientific models are not finished products but living representations of growing understanding. The 4-point peer review checklist operationalises the lesson's learning outcomes as observable, checkable criteria, embedding formative assessment directly into the student activity. Colour-coding plant-specific organelles in green creates a visual anchor that ties the diagram back to the sukuma wiki phenomenon.
framework.formativeAssessment: Observable indicator: Completed Version 1 diagrams include all 7 required plant cell labels and all 5 required animal cell labels, with NO cell wall or chloroplast on the animal cell diagram, and the central vacuole drawn large and centrally in the plant cell. Peer review checklists are completed and signed. Teacher collects 6–8 notebooks at the end of class to check diagram accuracy and peer review quality. Any notebook where the animal cell is drawn with a cell wall or the plant cell is missing a cell membrane is flagged for individual follow-up at the start of Lesson 5.

LESSONS.teacherReflection: After the lesson, reflect on the following questions: (1) During Phase 3 (Explain), did students make accurate and specific connections between each organelle and the anchoring phenomenon (blood smear + sukuma wiki cell), or did they complete the comparison table as a rote exercise? If the latter, plan a retrieval-practice opener for Lesson 5 using the phenomenon images. (2) How many 'STILL WONDERING' DQB cards in Phase 4 asked questions that signal misconceptions (e.g., confusing cell wall with cell membrane, or assuming chloroplasts are present in ALL plant cells, not just green parts)? List these misconceptions and address them explicitly at the start of Lesson 5. (3) In Phase 5 (Model Building), what were the three most common diagramming errors during circulation? Were they addressed in real time, or do they persist in the collected notebooks? Plan targeted corrections. (4) Was the wilting bean seedling demonstration (Phase 2) concrete and compelling enough to make turgor pressure intuitive? If students struggled with this concept, consider bringing in a bag of wilted vs. fresh sukuma wiki from a local market at the start of Lesson 5 for a brief tactile recall moment. (5) Did all learners — including those who may have missed Lesson 3 microscope observations — have sufficient prior evidence to engage meaningfully with the Explain phase? If not, what scaffolding can be provided at the start of Lesson 5?

summaryTablePrompt.observed: Students observed two bean seedlings — one turgid and upright (well-watered, like a healthy shamba in Kericho) and one wilted and drooping (water-deprived, like a farm during a dry spell in Machakos) — and connected the visible wilting to changes in the central vacuole inside plant cells. Students also revisited their own Lesson 3 microscope observation drawings of sukuma wiki leaf cells, identifying three structures absent in the blood smear from the anchoring phenomenon.
summaryTablePrompt.learned: Students learned that plant cells contain three organelles NOT found in animal cells: (1) Chloroplasts — green, oval organelles containing chlorophyll that capture sunlight to make food via photosynthesis, explaining why sukuma wiki is green and can produce its own glucose while blood cells cannot; (2) Cell Wall — a rigid outer layer made of cellulose outside the cell membrane, explaining why sukuma wiki leaf cells appear rectangular and have a defined shape, unlike the flexible, round red blood cells in the blood smear; (3) Central Vacuole — a large, water-filled sac that creates turgor pressure to keep plant cells firm and upright, explaining why a bean plant wilts when water is scarce and why the large central sac observed in Lesson 3 was pushing organelles to the edges of the sukuma wiki cell.
summaryTablePrompt.explained: Students can now partially explain the driving question phenomenon: the sukuma wiki leaf cell looks rectangular, green, and has a large central sac because it contains a cell wall (cellulose rigidity), chloroplasts (photosynthesis / green pigment), and a central vacuole (turgor pressure / water storage) — all of which are absent in the red blood cells and white blood cells seen in the Kisumu County Hospital blood smear. Despite these dramatic structural differences, both cell types share a cell membrane, cytoplasm, and nucleus (in most cases), confirming that they are both cells — but specialised for completely different jobs. Students have produced Version 1 of their cumulative 2D comparative cell diagrams, which they will continue to develop across Lessons 5–8.

LESSONS.aresKeywords: plant cell, animal cell, chloroplast, cell wall, central vacuole, turgor pressure, cellulose, photosynthesis, cell specialisation, organelle function, sukuma wiki, wilting, turgid, flaccid, blood smear, red blood cell, white blood cell, cell comparison, 2D cell diagram, cell biology Grade 10, Kenya CBE Biology, KICD strand 1.3

LESSONS.title: Are Bacteria Cells the Same as Our Cells? Prokaryotes vs. Eukaryotes

slo.purpose: To distinguish between prokaryotic and eukaryotic cells by examining structural evidence, and to explain how the absence or presence of membrane-bound organelles relates to cell complexity and function.
slo.knowledge: Learner knows that: (a) prokaryotic cells lack a membrane-bound nucleus and membrane-bound organelles; (b) eukaryotic cells possess a membrane-bound nucleus and specialised organelles; (c) bacteria are prokaryotes and are smaller in size than eukaryotic cells; (d) prokaryotes may have a cell wall, flagella, pili, and a nucleoid region; (e) all cells — prokaryote or eukaryote — share ribosomes, a cell membrane, cytoplasm, and DNA.
slo.skills: Learner is able to: (a) compare and contrast diagrams/images of E. coli (prokaryote) and a human cheek cell (eukaryote) using a structured comparison frame; (b) identify at least five structural differences between prokaryotic and eukaryotic cells; (c) revise a cumulative cell model to include a 'prokaryote column'; (d) use evidence to argue why the fermenting uji phenomenon involves prokaryotic rather than eukaryotic organisms.
slo.attitudes: Learner appreciates that even the simplest cells — lacking a nucleus — are remarkably well-adapted to perform complex biochemical work, and develops curiosity about microbial life in everyday Kenyan foods.
slo.keyInquiry: 1. What are the structural differences between plant and animal cells? 2. How does cell specialisation enable different cells to perform specific functions in living organisms?
slo.purposeInStoryline: Lessons 1–4 established what a 'generic' eukaryotic cell looks like and how specialised animal and plant cells deviate from that model (RBCs, WBCs, sukuma wiki leaf cells). Lesson 5 now confronts students with the deepest structural divide in all of biology: the prokaryote–eukaryote boundary. By revisiting the fermenting uji supporting phenomenon (bacteria causing fermentation), students discover that some cells have NO membrane-bound nucleus at all — shattering any assumption that 'all cells look basically alike.' This prepares students for Lesson 6, where they will synthesise all cell types into a unified explanatory model and address the driving question directly.
slo.safetyNotes: No live specimens or hazardous chemicals are used in this lesson. All observations are image/diagram-based. If a teacher demonstrates uji fermentation using a sealed jar, ensure the jar is not opened near students' faces — fermentation gases may cause discomfort. Remind learners to wash hands if they handle any food specimens. Supervise responsible internet/device use if digital microscopy images are accessed on ARES platform.

LESSONS.overview: In this OBSERVE + EXPLAIN lesson, students revisit the fermenting uji supporting phenomenon to surface a new question: what kind of cells are bacteria? Using annotated diagrams and images of E. coli alongside human cheek cells (eukaryote), students systematically compare prokaryotic and eukaryotic cell structures. Through a structured Claim–Evidence–Reasoning (CER) activity, learners identify the defining differences: no membrane-bound nucleus, no membrane-bound organelles, presence of a nucleoid region, cell wall, flagella, pili, and smaller overall size in prokaryotes. They then update their Driving Question Boards and revise their cumulative cell models to include a 'prokaryote column,' explicitly linking back to the anchoring phenomenon — why the cells in a blood smear, sukuma wiki, AND fermenting uji are all called 'cells' yet look so radically different.

framework.learnerExperience: Students are shown two stimulus prompts on the ARES platform: (1) a photograph of a bowl of fermenting uji (the supporting phenomenon from earlier lessons) with the caption 'Millions of bacteria are at work in this uji right now,' and (2) the anchoring phenomenon image — the blood smear showing RBCs, WBCs, and the sukuma wiki leaf cell. Students individually sketch in their exercise books what they predict a bacterial cell looks like compared to the human cheek cell they observed in Lesson 3. They annotate their sketch with at least THREE predicted features. Students then share predictions with a shoulder partner (Think-Pair) before a whole-class share-out.
framework.teacherMoves: Display the fermenting uji image and say: 'Before we look at any diagrams, I want you to use what you already know. In Lesson 3 you drew the human cheek cell. Now — in your book — sketch what you think a bacterial cell from this uji looks like. You have 3 minutes. Go.' [Allow 3 minutes of silent individual work. Circulate, noting 2–3 interesting or misconception-revealing sketches.] After 3 minutes: 'Now turn to your partner and share your sketch. What did you include? What did you leave out? Discuss for 2 minutes.' [Allow pair discussion.] Cold-call 3 students — choose one who drew a nucleus, one who did not, and one with an unusual feature. Ask each: 'Walk us through your sketch — what made you decide to draw it that way?' [WAIT TIME: 12 seconds after each question before accepting a response.] Record 3–4 key predicted features on the board under the heading 'OUR PREDICTIONS — Bacterial Cell.' Leave these visible throughout the lesson for later comparison.
framework.sensemakingStrategy: Think-Pair-Sketch: Students externalise prior knowledge by producing a drawing before instruction. This activates schema, surfaces misconceptions (e.g., 'bacteria have a nucleus like our cells'), and gives the teacher diagnostic information. Predictions remain on the board so students can experience genuine cognitive dissonance when evidence contradicts them.
framework.formativeAssessment: Teacher circulates during the 3-minute sketch and looks for: (a) whether students include a nucleus in the bacterial cell (a key misconception to address), (b) whether students reference any features from previous lessons (cell membrane, cell wall, organelles). Teacher mentally notes 1–2 students holding the 'bacteria have a nucleus' misconception to cold-call during the Explain phase for targeted correction.

framework.learnerExperience: Students receive (or access on ARES) a two-panel evidence sheet: Panel A shows a labelled diagram of an E. coli bacterium (prokaryote) with the following structures indicated: cell wall, cell membrane, cytoplasm, nucleoid region (circular DNA, NOT enclosed in a membrane), ribosomes (70S), flagellum, pili, and approximate size scale bar (~2 µm). Panel B shows a labelled diagram of a human cheek cell (eukaryote) with: cell membrane, cytoplasm, nucleus (with nuclear envelope), nucleolus, mitochondria, ribosomes (80S), endoplasmic reticulum, and approximate size scale bar (~20 µm). Students complete a structured Comparison Table in their exercise books with the following columns: 'Feature | E. coli (Prokaryote) | Human Cheek Cell (Eukaryote) | Present in BOTH / Only in Prokaryote / Only in Eukaryote.' They must fill in at least 10 features. After completing the table individually (8 minutes), students compare tables with their group of four and reconcile any differences.
framework.teacherMoves: Distribute or direct students to the ARES evidence sheet. Say: 'You are now a medical laboratory technologist — like the one in Kisumu who looked at the blood smear in our phenomenon. Your job is to study these two diagrams very carefully and fill in the comparison table. Every feature you can see, write it down. You have 8 minutes working individually — no talking yet.' [Set a visible timer. Circulate, specifically checking that students are noting the NUCLEOID region vs. NUCLEUS distinction — this is the conceptual heart of the lesson.] At 8 minutes: 'Now, in your group of four, compare your tables. If you disagree on whether a feature is present or absent, go back to the diagram — the diagram is your evidence.' [Allow 4 minutes of group reconciliation.] Bring class back together. Ask targeted questions to 4 different cold-called students: (1) 'Amara — what did you find in the E. coli cell where you would normally expect a nucleus?' [WAIT 12 seconds.] (2) 'Ochieng — how is the nucleoid region DIFFERENT from the nucleus in the cheek cell?' [WAIT 12 seconds.] (3) 'Wanjiku — look at your scale bars. How much bigger is the cheek cell compared to E. coli?' [WAIT 10 seconds.] (4) 'Kamau — both cells have ribosomes. Why do you think that is?' [WAIT 15 seconds.] Write student responses directly onto the class comparison table on the board.
framework.sensemakingStrategy: Structured Comparison Table (Evidence Sorting): Students sort observable features into categories — shared vs. unique — before the teacher explains the significance. This is an NGSS Science and Engineering Practice (Analysing and Interpreting Data). The physical act of categorising forces students to confront what is truly different rather than passively reading a list. The group reconciliation step builds scientific argumentation — students must use the diagram (evidence) to resolve disagreements.
framework.formativeAssessment: Teacher checks comparison tables during circulation for: (a) correct identification of 'nucleoid region' (not 'nucleus') in E. coli — this is the must-achieve indicator for this phase; (b) whether students have noted the size difference using the scale bars; (c) whether ribosomes appear in BOTH columns. Any student who writes 'nucleus' for E. coli is immediately redirected: 'Look again at the label on that structure — does it say nucleus or nucleoid? What do you notice about whether it has a surrounding membrane?' A quick show-of-hands — 'How many found at least 10 features?' — gives class-level data before moving to Explain.

framework.learnerExperience: Students write a Claim–Evidence–Reasoning (CER) paragraph in response to the following prompt displayed on ARES: 'The bacteria that ferment uji (like Lactobacillus) are prokaryotes. A Form 1 student says: "Bacteria cannot be real cells because they have no nucleus — the nucleus is what makes something a cell." Using your comparison table as evidence, write a CER response that either supports or refutes this claim. Your response must: (1) state a clear Claim, (2) cite at least THREE pieces of structural evidence from today's diagrams, (3) provide Reasoning that connects each piece of evidence to your claim, and (4) explain what ALL cells — prokaryote and eukaryote alike — have in common.' Students write individually (10 minutes), then two volunteers share their CER with the class for peer critique using the 'Glow and Grow' protocol (one strength, one suggestion).
framework.teacherMoves: Display the CER prompt and say: 'Now it is time to use your evidence to think like a scientist. Read the prompt carefully. You are going to write a CER paragraph — Claim, Evidence, Reasoning. Remember: Evidence comes from the diagram, not from what you feel is true. You have 10 minutes.' [Circulate purposefully. Look for students who are only writing a claim with no evidence — prompt them: 'Where in your comparison table is the proof for that? Point to it.' Also look for students who cite only one piece of evidence — say: 'KICD says scientists use multiple lines of evidence. Can you find two more features that support your claim?'] After 10 minutes, ask for two volunteers to read their CER aloud. After each reading, facilitate peer feedback: 'Give one GLOW — something they did well — and one GROW — something they could add or clarify.' [WAIT 10 seconds after asking for glow/grow before accepting responses. Cold-call if no volunteers.] Synthesise on the board: 'So what do ALL cells share, no matter what?' Guide students to articulate: cell membrane, cytoplasm, DNA (in some form), ribosomes. Write these four as the 'Universal Cell Features.' Then say: 'The bacteria in the uji do not need a membrane-bound nucleus to carry out the chemistry of life — their DNA is free in the cytoplasm. This is why we call them PROkaryotes — from the Greek for BEFORE the nucleus.' Write etymology on board. Explicitly connect back to the phenomenon: 'Remember our Kisumu student looking at the blood smear? The RBCs had NO nucleus either — but RBCs are eukaryotic cells that LOST their nucleus as they specialised. That is very different from bacteria, which NEVER had a membrane-bound nucleus to begin with. This distinction matters.'
framework.sensemakingStrategy: Claim–Evidence–Reasoning (CER) Writing: CER is an NGSS Science and Engineering Practice (Engaging in Argument from Evidence). It requires students to explicitly link observational data to conceptual conclusions, preventing the common error of asserting knowledge without grounding it in evidence. The 'Glow and Grow' peer critique adds an evaluative layer, pushing students to assess the quality of scientific arguments — a higher-order thinking skill aligned to CBC's Communication and Collaboration competency.
framework.formativeAssessment: Teacher reads 3–4 CER responses during circulation. Must-achieve indicator: student cites 'no membrane-bound nucleus' and 'nucleoid region' as specific structural evidence — not just 'bacteria are different.' If a student's CER only states the claim with no structural evidence, teacher prompts: 'Your claim is strong — now find three rows in your comparison table where E. coli and the cheek cell are different, and quote them.' Exit check: after the two volunteers share, teacher asks the whole class by show of hands: 'How many of you included the word nucleoid in your evidence?' This reveals whether the lesson's core concept has landed before moving to model building.

framework.learnerExperience: Students return to the class Driving Question Board. Each group receives two sticky notes of different colours: one colour for NEW ANSWERS/EVIDENCE (green) and one colour for NEW QUESTIONS RAISED (orange). Groups discuss for 3 minutes: 'What did today's lesson answer from our DQB? What new questions has it raised?' Groups post their sticky notes on the DQB. The teacher then facilitates a 5-minute class discussion to cluster new questions into themes. Students also update the 'Evidence We Have So Far' section of their individual Science Notebooks with a one-sentence summary: 'Today I learned that…'
framework.teacherMoves: Distribute sticky notes and say: 'Go to your group. Look at our Driving Question Board. In 3 minutes, decide: (1) Which question on the board can we now answer — or partly answer — using today's evidence? Write the answer on your GREEN note. (2) What NEW question has today's lesson raised for you? Write it on your ORANGE note. Go.' [Allow 3 minutes of group discussion. Circulate, prompting groups who are stuck: 'Think about the original phenomenon — the blood smear, the sukuma wiki. Does today's lesson help explain anything new about why those cells look different from each other?'] After 3 minutes, have each group post their notes. Read 2–3 green notes aloud and affirm: 'This group is saying today's lesson answers the question about whether bacteria are the same as our cells — and they are providing the structural reason. Excellent.' Read 1–2 orange notes and say: 'These new questions are exactly what scientists ask. Look — someone is asking whether viruses are prokaryotes or eukaryotes. That is a fantastic extension question. Let us park it here.' Circle 'parked' questions in a designated DQB corner. End by asking: 'Has today's lesson brought us closer to answering our DRIVING question — why do the cells in your body and in sukuma wiki look so different? Thumbs up, sideways, or down.' [WAIT 8 seconds. Scan the room.] Say: 'Hold that thought — we are building toward the full answer in Lesson 6.'
framework.sensemakingStrategy: Driving Question Board (DQB) Update with Colour-Coded Sticky Notes: The DQB is a running public record of the class's growing understanding. Colour-coding answers vs. new questions makes the epistemic progress visible — students can literally see that science generates new questions even as it answers old ones. This strategy builds metacognition (students reflect on what they now know vs. what remains unknown) and sustains intellectual momentum across the 8-lesson sequence.
framework.formativeAssessment: Teacher reads all posted sticky notes during the 5-minute discussion. Green notes that correctly cite 'no membrane-bound nucleus' or 'prokaryote vs. eukaryote' as a new answer indicate understanding has been achieved. Green notes that are vague ('bacteria are smaller') indicate surface-level understanding — teacher makes note to probe these students in Lesson 6. Orange notes that generate meaningful extension questions (e.g., 'Do all bacteria have flagella?' or 'If RBCs have no nucleus, are they prokaryotes?') indicate high engagement and readiness for synthesis. Teacher photographs the updated DQB for reference in Lesson 6 planning.

framework.learnerExperience: Students retrieve their cumulative cell model from Lesson 4 (a three-column comparison chart: Generic Eukaryotic Cell | Animal Specialised Cells | Plant Specialised Cells). They now add a fourth column: 'Prokaryotic Cell (e.g., Lactobacillus in uji / E. coli).' Using coloured pens or pencils, students annotate this column with: (a) a labelled sketch of a prokaryotic cell including nucleoid region, cell wall, cell membrane, cytoplasm, ribosomes, flagellum, and pili; (b) colour-coding to show which features are SHARED with eukaryotes (use the same colour as in previous columns) and which are UNIQUE to prokaryotes (a new colour). Students also add a 'Universal Cell Features' row at the TOP of their model, listing the four features all cells share (cell membrane, cytoplasm, DNA, ribosomes). Finally, students write one sentence below their model connecting it to the anchoring phenomenon: 'This helps explain the phenomenon because…'
framework.teacherMoves: Say: 'Take out your cumulative cell model from Lesson 4. Today we are going to make it more powerful by adding a prokaryote column. This model is growing lesson by lesson — by Lesson 8, it will help you fully answer our driving question.' [Allow students to retrieve models — 1 minute.] 'First, add a fourth column header: Prokaryotic Cell — and write the example: Lactobacillus from fermenting uji. Now sketch and label the E. coli diagram from today's evidence sheet into that column. Use the same colour-coding system we established in Lesson 2 — shared features in BLUE, unique features in RED. You have 7 minutes.' [Circulate. Check that students are drawing the nucleoid region WITHOUT a surrounding membrane envelope — correct any student who draws a membrane around the DNA in the prokaryote column: 'Remember — PRO means before — there is no membrane surrounding the DNA in a prokaryote.'] After 7 minutes: 'Now add the Universal Cell Features row at the very top of your model. What four things do ALL cells — prokaryote and eukaryote — share?' [Cold-call 4 students, one per feature. WAIT 10 seconds each.] 'Finally — the most important part — write one sentence at the bottom: THIS HELPS EXPLAIN THE PHENOMENON BECAUSE… Connect your model to the Kisumu blood smear or the sukuma wiki or the uji. You have 2 minutes.' [After 2 minutes, cold-call 2 students to share their sentence. Write the best class synthesis sentence on the board.]
framework.sensemakingStrategy: Cumulative Model Revision (NGSS Developing and Using Models): The growing model is the central sensemaking artefact of the entire 8-lesson sequence. Each lesson adds a column or layer, making students' conceptual understanding literally visible and revisable. Adding the prokaryote column forces students to restructure — not just add to — their existing model, which is the hallmark of genuine conceptual change rather than additive memorisation. The colour-coding system (consistent across all lessons) allows students to instantly see patterns of shared and unique features across all cell types.
framework.formativeAssessment: Teacher checks 5–6 models during the 7-minute sketch period. Must-achieve indicators: (a) nucleoid region is drawn WITHOUT a membrane envelope; (b) cell wall is present in the prokaryote column; (c) no mitochondria or ER appear in the prokaryote column; (d) ribosomes appear in BOTH prokaryote and eukaryote columns. The phenomenon-connection sentence is a key formative indicator — a sentence like 'This helps explain the phenomenon because the bacteria in the uji are prokaryotes that have DNA but no nucleus, which is completely different from the WBCs in the blood smear that have large visible nuclei' demonstrates full lesson integration. A sentence like 'This explains cells' indicates surface engagement and warrants follow-up in the next lesson.

LESSONS.teacherReflection: After the lesson, reflect on the following: (1) CONCEPTUAL DEPTH — Did students consistently and correctly distinguish 'nucleoid region' (no membrane) from 'nucleus' (membrane-bound) in their CER writing and model? If the majority were still conflating these, the Explain phase needs a more explicit direct comparison using a physical analogy (e.g., 'Imagine your DNA is a book. In a eukaryotic cell, that book is locked in a library — the nucleus. In a prokaryotic cell, the book is lying open on a table in the middle of the room — accessible, but not enclosed'). (2) PHENOMENON CONNECTION — Did students' DQB contributions and model sentences genuinely link back to the anchoring phenomenon (blood smear / sukuma wiki / uji), or were responses generic? If connections were weak, begin Lesson 6 by re-displaying the anchoring phenomenon image and explicitly asking: 'Where do prokaryotes appear in THIS picture?' (3) CER QUALITY — Were students citing specific structural features as evidence (e.g., 'no membrane-bound nucleus,' 'presence of nucleoid region,' 'no mitochondria') or were they writing vague claims? If CER quality was low, build in a sentence-starter scaffold in Lesson 6: 'My evidence is [specific feature] from [diagram panel], which shows that…' (4) PACE — The lesson has significant content. If Phase 2 (comparison table) ran over time, consider providing a partially pre-filled table in future iterations, leaving only 5 features for students to complete independently rather than 10. (5) EQUITY — Were there students who did not contribute during cold-calls? Review your cold-call list and ensure all students — including those who are quieter or who struggle with English as a medium — are called on with scaffolded questions ('Tell me ONE thing you see that is different between the two cells') rather than open-ended synthesis questions.

summaryTablePrompt.observed: Students examined labelled diagrams of E. coli (prokaryote) and a human cheek cell (eukaryote) on the ARES evidence sheet, and completed a structured 10-feature comparison table identifying features present in both, only in prokaryotes, or only in eukaryotes.
summaryTablePrompt.learned: Prokaryotic cells (e.g., bacteria like Lactobacillus in fermenting uji) have no membrane-bound nucleus and no membrane-bound organelles; their DNA sits free in the cytoplasm in a region called the nucleoid. Eukaryotic cells (e.g., human cheek cells, sukuma wiki leaf cells) have a membrane-bound nucleus and specialised organelles. Despite these differences, ALL cells share four universal features: a cell membrane, cytoplasm, DNA, and ribosomes.
summaryTablePrompt.explained: Using a Claim–Evidence–Reasoning paragraph, students explained that bacteria ARE real cells — despite lacking a nucleus — because they possess all four universal cell features. Students connected this to the anchoring phenomenon by recognising that the bacteria fermenting uji are prokaryotes whose structural simplicity (no nucleus, no membrane-bound organelles) is fundamentally different from the nuclear loss seen in specialised eukaryotic RBCs, and updated their cumulative cell models to include a prokaryote column alongside the generic eukaryotic, specialised animal, and specialised plant cell columns.

LESSONS.aresKeywords: prokaryote, eukaryote, nucleoid region, nucleus, cell wall, E. coli, Lactobacillus, fermenting uji, cell membrane, ribosomes, flagellum, pili, CER writing, comparison table, cumulative cell model, Driving Question Board, cell specialisation, universal cell features, Kenya Grade 10 Biology, Sub-Strand 1.3

LESSONS.title: Cell Specialisation — Why Don't All Cells Look the Same?
LESSONS.aresKeywords: cell specialisation, red blood cells, guard cells, root hair cells, muscle cells, nerve cells, sperm cells, haemoglobin, stomata, surface area, sickle cell anaemia, structure-function relationship

slo.purpose: To explain how the structure of specialised cells is directly related to the specific function each cell performs in a living organism.
slo.knowledge: By the end of the lesson, the learner should be able to: (a) identify the structural features of six specialised cells — red blood cells, guard cells, root hair cells, muscle cells, nerve cells, and sperm cells; (b) explain how each structural feature enables the cell to carry out its specific function; (c) relate the abnormal biconcave shape of sickle cells to the disruption of oxygen-transport function.
slo.skills: By the end of the lesson, the learner should be able to: (a) analyse evidence cards to match structural features to cellular functions; (b) construct a written structure-function argument using scientific reasoning; (c) revise a cumulative cell model to incorporate specialisation as a deviation from the generic cell.
slo.attitudes: The learner appreciates that diversity in cell structure is not random but purposefully adapted, and develops curiosity about how microscopic structures explain macroscopic health outcomes such as sickle cell anaemia.
slo.keyInquiry: How does cell specialisation enable different cells to perform specific functions in living organisms?
slo.purposeInStoryline: In Lessons 1–5 students built and refined a generic cell model and gathered evidence on organelle functions. Lesson 6 is the EXPLAIN phase: students now use that foundation to account for WHY specialised cells deviate from the generic model. By the end of this lesson, students can answer the second half of the driving question — 'how does each cell's structure allow it to do its specific job?' — and revisit the anchoring phenomenon (blood smear, sukuma wiki leaf cell, sickle cells) with a full explanatory model.
slo.safetyNotes: No hazardous materials are used in this lesson. If printed evidence cards are laminated, remind students to handle edges carefully. If a brief microscope observation of a prepared slide is included as an extension, follow standard laboratory rules: carry microscopes with two hands, do not touch the objective lens, report any cracked slides immediately to the teacher.

LESSONS.overview: Students enter a lesson anchored in the original blood-smear and sukuma wiki phenomenon. Working in groups of four, they receive a set of six Evidence Cards — one per specialised cell (red blood cell, guard cell, root hair cell, muscle cell, nerve cell, sperm cell) — each card containing a labelled diagram, a Kenya-relevant context clue, and a partial function statement. Through a structured 'Structure–Function Match' activity, groups complete the function statements by identifying which structural feature makes the function possible. A whole-class 'Claim–Evidence–Reasoning' (CER) share-out consolidates understanding. The lesson culminates in students returning to the sickle-cell supporting phenomenon to explain — using their new vocabulary — why the crescent shape of a sickle cell disrupts haemoglobin packing and oxygen transport. Students then update their cumulative cell models on the Driving Question Board, adding a 'Specialisation Arrow' showing how generic cells diverge into specialised forms.

framework.learnerExperience: Students are shown two side-by-side images on the board: (A) the blood smear from the Kisumu county hospital — showing a biconcave red blood cell and a white blood cell with a large nucleus — and (B) a cross-section of a sukuma wiki leaf cell with a visible chloroplast and central vacuole. Each student independently writes a response to the prompt: 'We have studied the generic cell. These cells are NOT generic. List THREE things that are different about each cell and make a prediction: why might each difference exist?' Students record predictions in their science notebooks. No peer discussion yet — this is individual thinking time.
framework.teacherMoves: Display the two images and read the prompt aloud. Say: 'You have 3 minutes of silent thinking time — no talking, no sharing yet. Write down everything you notice that is DIFFERENT from our generic cell model.' START a visible 3-minute timer. Circulate and observe notebooks — do NOT correct or affirm yet. After 3 minutes, cold-call 4 students (aim for gender balance and at least one student who has been quieter in prior lessons): 'Tell me ONE difference you wrote down — just one.' Record each response verbatim on the board in a column labelled 'Our Predictions.' After all 4 responses, say: 'Hold those predictions — we are going to test every single one of them today using evidence cards. By the end of this lesson you will know whether your prediction was right, partially right, or needs revision.' Do NOT confirm or deny any prediction.
framework.sensemakingStrategy: Individual Prediction Writing — This strategy activates prior knowledge from Lessons 1–5 (generic cell model) and surfaces student misconceptions before instruction. Recording predictions publicly on the board creates accountability and gives students a personal stake in the evidence-gathering that follows.
framework.formativeAssessment: Circulate and scan notebooks during the 3-minute silent period. Look for: (a) students who correctly identify absence of nucleus in RBCs, (b) students who notice the chloroplast or vacuole in the leaf cell. Students who write only vague observations ('they look different') will need targeted prompting in Phase 2. Cold-call responses on the board give a quick whole-class snapshot of prior knowledge depth.

framework.learnerExperience: Groups of four receive a set of six colour-coded Evidence Cards spread face-down on their desk. Each card contains: (1) a labelled diagram of the specialised cell, (2) a Kenya-relevant context clue (e.g., 'This cell is found in the blood of a marathon runner from Eldoret — it must carry oxygen to muscles for 42 km'), and (3) a partial function statement with a blank: 'This cell can __________ because its structure includes __________.' The six cells are: RED BLOOD CELL (biconcave disc, no nucleus — Kisumu blood donation context); GUARD CELL (bean-shaped pair, chloroplasts — sukuma wiki shamba context); ROOT HAIR CELL (long hair-like extension — bean seedling germinating in Rift Valley soil context); MUSCLE CELL (elongated, striations, many mitochondria — Kenyan athlete sprinting context); NERVE CELL (long axon, myelin sheath, dendrites — signal from finger to brain after touching a hot sufuria context); SPERM CELL (compact head, midpiece packed with mitochondria, long flagellum — fertilisation context). Groups flip cards one at a time, read them aloud within the group, examine the diagram, and discuss: 'What structural features do you see? What might they be for?' Each student records observations in their notebook using a two-column table: STRUCTURE | POSSIBLE FUNCTION.
framework.teacherMoves: Distribute evidence card envelopes before the phase begins. Say: 'You will work as a group of four. Roles today: Card Reader (reads the context clue aloud), Diagram Analyst (describes what they see in the diagram), Recorder (fills in the two-column table for the group), and Devil's Advocate (challenges the group — asks WHY after every suggestion). Rotate roles for each new card.' Allow 12 minutes for group evidence examination. Circulate to every group at least once. Use these specific probing questions when you crouch beside a group: For RBC card — 'If there is no nucleus, what happened to that space? What might fill it instead?' (WAIT 10 seconds before accepting a response.) For guard cell card — 'These cells are in sukuma wiki. What do plants need to let in and out? How might a bean shape help with that?' (WAIT 12 seconds.) For root hair card — 'Imagine you are a water molecule in the soil near a bean seedling in Rift Valley. How does this shape make it easier for the cell to absorb you?' (WAIT 10 seconds.) Cold-call at least 6 different students across all groups during circulations — not just willing volunteers. After 12 minutes, give a 1-minute warning: 'Finish your last card and make sure your Recorder has something written for all six cells.'
framework.sensemakingStrategy: Evidence Card Analysis with Structured Roles — Assigning rotating roles (Reader, Diagram Analyst, Recorder, Devil's Advocate) ensures all four group members engage with every card rather than one student dominating. The two-column STRUCTURE | POSSIBLE FUNCTION table scaffolds the structure-function reasoning that is the core scientific practice of this lesson (NGSS Science and Engineering Practice 6: Constructing Explanations). The Kenya-relevant context clues (Eldoret marathon runner, Kisumu blood donation, sukuma wiki shamba, bean seedling, hot sufuria) connect abstract cellular biology to lived experience, making the evidence personally meaningful.
framework.formativeAssessment: During circulation, check that the two-column tables contain specific structural vocabulary (e.g., 'no nucleus,' 'elongated,' 'flagellum,' 'many mitochondria') rather than vague terms ('looks weird,' 'has a tail'). Groups that use only vague language need the teacher to model one example: point to the diagram and say, 'What is the NAME of this structure here — do you remember it from our generic cell model?' This links back to Lesson 4 organelle vocabulary. A group is ready for Phase 3 when at least 4 of their 6 rows contain a specific structural feature matched to a plausible function.

framework.learnerExperience: Part A — CER Share-Out (12 min): Each group selects ONE evidence card to present to the class using the Claim–Evidence–Reasoning (CER) framework written on the board: CLAIM: 'The [cell name] can [function] because…' | EVIDENCE: 'Its structure includes [specific feature], as shown in the diagram by [describe]…' | REASONING: 'This feature helps it function because [scientific explanation]…' Each group has 90 seconds to present. Other groups listen and use a 'Plus/Delta' sticky note: Plus = one thing the presenting group got right; Delta = one thing to add or correct. Part B — Sickle Cell Revisit (8 min): Teacher returns to the anchoring phenomenon. Students are shown three images side by side: a normal biconcave RBC, a sickle-shaped RBC, and a diagram of a blocked blood capillary. Students write a 3-sentence CER explanation in their notebooks answering: 'Why does the sickle shape prevent the red blood cell from doing its job?' Part C — Whole-Class Debrief (5 min): Teacher collects 3 CER responses (cold-called, not volunteered) and reads them aloud. Class votes: 'Does this explanation include a CLAIM? Does it include EVIDENCE from the diagram? Does it include REASONING that links structure to function?' Thumbs up / thumbs sideways / thumbs down for each criterion.
framework.teacherMoves: For Part A: Write the CER scaffold on the board before the phase begins. Say: 'Every group will present ONE card using this exact structure — Claim, Evidence, Reasoning. You have 2 minutes to decide which card and prepare your 90 seconds.' Use a timer visibly. After each group presents, facilitate Plus/Delta: 'Group 2, hold your sticky notes — what is your Plus for Group 1?' Cold-call the Plus from one group and the Delta from a different group. Do NOT editorialize — say: 'Thank you. Group 1, does that Delta change anything about your reasoning?' (WAIT 8 seconds for Group 1 to respond.) Ensure all 6 specialised cells are covered across the 6 groups — if two groups chose the same card, redirect: 'Group 4, Group 2 already took the nerve cell — choose a different one.' For Part B: Project the three sickle-cell images. Say: 'You have studied normal red blood cells. You now see what a sickle cell looks like. Using ONLY what you know about structure and function, write three sentences. Use the words: shape, haemoglobin, oxygen, capillary.' (WAIT — allow 5 minutes of silent individual writing.) For Part C: Do NOT call on raised hands. Say: 'I am going to read three responses — Amina, Brian, and Wanjiru, please hand me your notebooks.' Read each response aloud neutrally. For each, ask the class to evaluate against the three CER criteria using thumbs. After all three, say: 'What would make the STRONGEST version of this explanation? What is missing?' Cold-call 2 students for additions.
framework.sensemakingStrategy: Claim–Evidence–Reasoning (CER) Framework — CER is an explicit argumentation scaffold (NGSS Science and Engineering Practice 7: Engaging in Argument from Evidence) that separates the three components of a scientific explanation. By requiring students to name the specific structural feature as their EVIDENCE and then articulate the mechanistic link as their REASONING, CER prevents the common misconception that correlation equals explanation (e.g., 'it has no nucleus so it carries oxygen' vs. 'the absence of a nucleus creates more internal space, which allows more haemoglobin molecules to pack in, increasing oxygen-carrying capacity'). The sickle-cell revisit is a deliberate transfer task — students must apply the same structure-function logic to an ABNORMAL case, deepening understanding of the norm by examining its disruption.
framework.formativeAssessment: Collect 6 Plus/Delta sticky notes after Part A — these reveal whether students can identify correct vs. incomplete structure-function reasoning in peers' explanations. For Part B, use a rapid 4-point rubric during cold-call reading: 1 = claim only, no structure named; 2 = claim + structure named; 3 = claim + structure + function linked; 4 = claim + structure + mechanism explained (e.g., 'sickle shape causes cells to stack and block narrow capillaries, reducing oxygen delivery to tissues'). Students scoring 1–2 need a targeted follow-up question: 'You said the shape causes a problem — what physical thing does the sickle shape do inside a blood vessel?' Students scoring 4 can be invited to add their reasoning to the board as the class model answer.

framework.learnerExperience: Students return to the class Driving Question Board (DQB) — a large display that has been accumulating sticky notes, arrows, and model revisions since Lesson 1. Each student receives two sticky notes: one ORANGE ('I can now explain…') and one BLUE ('I still wonder…'). Students write one statement on each. They then approach the DQB and place their notes in the designated columns. The teacher facilitates a 3-minute whole-class gallery walk of the new sticky notes. Students look specifically for: (a) a 'still wonder' question that connects cell specialisation to the next topic (Chemicals of Life / Strand 1.4), and (b) any 'I can now explain' note that directly addresses the original driving question. The class votes on the single most important remaining question to carry forward.
framework.teacherMoves: Distribute sticky notes and say: 'ORANGE note: complete this sentence — I can now explain [blank] about why cells look different. Be SPECIFIC — name a cell and its feature.' (WAIT 2 minutes of silent writing.) 'BLUE note: What do you still wonder? What does this lesson NOT yet explain about the driving question?' (WAIT 2 minutes.) Direct students to post their notes. During the gallery walk, say: 'Read at least 5 notes that are NOT your own. If you see a BLUE note that matches something you also wonder, put a small tick on it.' After 3 minutes, facilitate a quick vote: 'Which BLUE note asks the most important question we haven't answered yet?' Read the top-voted question aloud and say: 'We will keep this question live on our DQB. In Strand 1.4, when we study the chemicals of life, you may find part of the answer — watch for it.' Write the question on a dedicated 'Carry-Forward' card and pin it prominently.
framework.sensemakingStrategy: Driving Question Board (DQB) with Orange/Blue Sticky Notes — The DQB is a visible, cumulative knowledge artefact. The two-colour system distinguishes consolidated understanding (orange) from productive uncertainty (blue). Publicly posting 'still wonder' questions normalises scientific uncertainty as a driver of inquiry rather than a failure of learning. The tick-voting system reveals shared gaps in understanding that the teacher can use to plan Lessons 7–8 and the transition to Strand 1.4. The Carry-Forward question bridges sub-strands, supporting curriculum coherence.
framework.formativeAssessment: Read all orange sticky notes before Lesson 7. Categorise them: (A) correctly links a specific structural feature to a specific function — these students are ready for Lesson 7 extension; (B) names a cell but does not name the structural feature — these students need a review prompt at the start of Lesson 7; (C) gives only a generic statement ('I can explain cells') — flag these students for a brief 1-on-1 check-in. Count the proportion of blue 'still wonder' notes that ask mechanistic questions ('HOW does…' / 'WHY does…') versus descriptive questions ('WHAT does… look like'). A class with more than 50% mechanistic blue notes is demonstrating strong scientific thinking and is ready for the model-building phase.

framework.learnerExperience: Students open their cumulative cell model — begun in Lesson 2 and revised in Lessons 3, 4, and 5. They add a 'Specialisation Layer' to their model. Using a new colour of pen, each student draws a 'divergence arrow' from their generic cell and sketches TWO specialised cells of their choice (from the six studied today), labelling: (a) the structural feature that differs from the generic cell, (b) the function that feature enables, and (c) a Kenya-relevant context sentence (e.g., 'In a sukuma wiki guard cell, the bean shape allows the stomatal pore to open and close, controlling water loss from the shamba plant'). Students who finish early add a third specialised cell and annotate how the sickle cell mutation represents an UNINTENDED structural change that disrupts function. At the end of the phase, students hold up their models for a brief 'gallery share' — they compare their divergence arrows with a partner and discuss: 'Did we choose the same structural feature to highlight? Did we use the same or different Kenya context?'
framework.teacherMoves: Say: 'Open your cumulative model. Pick up your green pen — green means SPECIALISATION. You are going to add a Specialisation Layer. You MUST: draw two divergence arrows from your generic cell, sketch two specialised cells, label the structural feature in green, and write one Kenya sentence for each.' Display a model exemplar on the board (teacher-drawn, intentionally incomplete — missing the Kenya sentence) and say: 'My model has a gap — what is missing?' (WAIT 10 seconds — cold-call 1 student.) 'Correct — I have not written my Kenya context sentence. Why does that matter?' (WAIT 8 seconds — cold-call 1 different student.) Allow 10 minutes of independent drawing. At the 8-minute mark, give a 2-minute warning: 'Finish labelling your second cell.' For the gallery share, say: 'Hold your model next to your partner's. You have 2 minutes — find ONE thing that is the same and ONE thing that is different about how you both drew the specialisation. Be ready to share.' Cold-call 2 pairs to share their comparison.
